title: Learning Latency-Aware Orchestration for Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Multi-agent systems (MAS) coordinate multiple LLM-powered agents through structured workflows, gaining reasoning power but incurring high inference latency from multi-step execution and repeated model invocations. Existing orchestration methods primarily optimize task performance and inference cost, leaving latency largely unaddressed. In MAS, end-to-end latency is governed by the critical execution path, so reducing total cost alone does not reliably reduce latency. Moreover, optimizing latency while preserving accuracy remains non-trivial: naive latency optimization can misassign operator-level credit and degrade task accuracy. To address this gap, we propose Latency-Aware Multi-agent System (LAMaS), a latency-aware orchestration framework for learning-based multi-agent systems. LAMaS addresses this challenge at two levels: at training time, it learns latency-aware execution graphs through constrained optimization with critical-path-aware credit assignment; at inference time, since a graph committed at training time cannot exploit runtime evidence, it complements graph construction with a lightweight controller that adaptively eliminates redundant future agent interactions as execution unfolds. Experiments on four benchmarks show that LAMaS achieves the best latency among evaluated learning-based MAS baselines, reducing end-to-end latency by over 50\% while maintaining competitive or better accuracy. LAMaS is also modular and transfers to other MAS with minimal changes, consistently yielding latency reductions.

AI.tldr: LAMaS is a latency-aware orchestration framework for multi-agent LLM systems that reduces inference latency by 50%+ while maintaining accuracy through critical-path-aware training and runtime adaptive control.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems incur high inference latency from multi-step execution and repeated model invocations. Existing methods optimize task performance and cost but largely ignore latency, and naive latency optimization can degrade accuracy.
AI.method: Two-level approach: (1) At training time, learns latency-aware execution graphs through constrained optimization with critical-path-aware credit assignment; (2) At inference time, uses lightweight controller to adaptively eliminate redundant future agent interactions as execution unfolds.
AI.result: Experiments on four benchmarks show LAMaS achieves best latency among learning-based MAS baselines, reducing end-to-end latency by over 50% while maintaining competitive or better accuracy. Modular and transferable to other MAS.
AI.conclusion: LAMaS effectively addresses the latency-accuracy trade-off in multi-agent LLM systems through a principled framework that optimizes for latency while preserving task performance.

title: Equilibrium stability as a driver of cooperation among Q-learners
summary: Algorithmic collusion among pricing algorithms has raised concerns about sustained supra-competitive prices and their implications for social welfare. Existing work has largely focused on the probability that reinforcement-learning algorithms converge to cooperative strategies, typically under the assumption that exploration vanishes over time. Motivated by the observation that algorithms deployed in practice are likely to continue exploring in order to remain adaptive to changing environments, we study learning dynamics under constant exploration. In this setting, the relevant question is no longer whether an algorithm converges to a particular strategy profile, but rather what fraction of time the algorithms spend playing cooperative strategies. Even in the benchmark case of the repeated Prisoner's Dilemma with one-period memory, this yields high-dimensional stochastic learning dynamics, for which a complete analytic treatment is intractable. We show that cooperative strategies can be dominant in this time-averaged sense and derive a boundary predicting when such dominance arises, based on the expected dynamics of the Q-learning process. Extensive simulations show that this boundary is a strong predictor for non-defection-dominated behaviour under epsilon-greedy Q-learning.

AI.tldr: Algorithms with constant exploration (not vanishing) can still spend significant time in cooperative strategies in repeated Prisoner's Dilemma, with a derived boundary predicting when cooperation dominates.
AI.motivation: Real-world pricing algorithms likely continue exploring to adapt to changing environments, unlike theoretical models assuming vanishing exploration. This raises the question of how much time algorithms spend in cooperative strategies rather than whether they converge to them.
AI.method: Studies epsilon-greedy Q-learning with constant exploration in repeated Prisoner's Dilemma with one-period memory. Analyzes stochastic learning dynamics through expected dynamics of Q-learning process and extensive simulations to validate predictions.
AI.result: Derived a boundary that predicts when cooperative strategies dominate in time-averaged sense. Simulations show this boundary strongly predicts non-defection-dominated behavior under epsilon-greedy Q-learning.
AI.conclusion: Cooperative strategies can dominate even with constant exploration, contrary to vanishing exploration assumptions. The derived boundary provides a practical predictor for when algorithmic collusion is likely to emerge in real-world settings where algorithms continue exploring.

title: Extending Liquid Rank Toward Multi-Source Reputation Aggregation
summary: In this paper, we present an extension of liquid rank reputation systems that enables the aggregation and blending of multiple heterogeneous reputation sources into a unified reputation score. The proposed framework supports the incorporation of external reputational signals alongside internally generated reputation, allowing influence to reflect participation and contribution across multiple contexts and subsystems. By introducing explicit weighting and blending mechanisms, the model provides fine-grained control over the relative impact of individual reputation sources, making it adaptable to diverse governance and coordination scenarios involving both human and machine agents. The resulting approach extends existing liquid rank systems and offers a flexible foundation for designing reputation-based governance mechanisms in complex socio-technical environments.

AI.tldr: Extension of liquid rank reputation systems that aggregates multiple heterogeneous reputation sources into unified scores with explicit weighting and blending mechanisms for governance applications.
AI.motivation: Existing liquid rank systems need to handle multiple reputation sources, both internal and external, to support complex governance scenarios involving diverse contexts and subsystems where influence should reflect participation across different domains.
AI.method: Propose a framework that extends liquid rank systems with aggregation and blending mechanisms, allowing incorporation of heterogeneous reputation sources with explicit weighting controls to manage relative impact of different reputation signals.
AI.result: A flexible reputation system that can unify multiple reputation sources into coherent scores, providing fine-grained control over reputation blending and adaptability to various governance scenarios with human and machine agents.
AI.conclusion: The extended liquid rank framework offers a foundation for designing reputation-based governance mechanisms in complex socio-technical environments by enabling the integration of diverse reputation sources with configurable influence.

title: Social Simulations: from Agent-Based Modeling to Digital Twins
comment: Entry for Encyclopedia of Social Network Analysis and Mining
summary: This book chapter covers the evolution of social simulation from classical agent-based models, in which agents interact according to explicitly defined behavioral rules, to AI-enhanced simulations based on Large Language Models and, ultimately, Social Digital Twins: high-fidelity, data-driven representations of real-world socio-technical systems. Along this trajectory, we discuss the main methodological foundations, applications, advantages, and limitations of each paradigm, highlighting the progressive shift from abstract models designed to investigate general social mechanisms toward increasingly realistic computational representations of specific social systems.

AI.tldr: The paper traces the evolution of social simulation from classical agent-based models to AI-enhanced simulations using Large Language Models, culminating in Social Digital Twins as high-fidelity, data-driven representations of real-world socio-technical systems.
AI.motivation: To document and analyze the progression of social simulation methodologies from traditional approaches to cutting-edge AI-enhanced techniques, examining how the field has evolved toward more realistic computational representations of social systems.
AI.method: The authors provide a comprehensive review and analysis of three paradigms: 1) classical agent-based models with explicitly defined behavioral rules, 2) AI-enhanced simulations based on Large Language Models, and 3) Social Digital Twins as high-fidelity, data-driven representations.
AI.result: The paper presents a trajectory showing a progressive shift from abstract models investigating general social mechanisms toward increasingly realistic computational representations of specific social systems, highlighting the methodological foundations, applications, advantages, and limitations of each approach.
AI.conclusion: Social simulation has evolved significantly from classical agent-based models to AI-enhanced approaches and Social Digital Twins, representing a shift toward higher fidelity and data-driven realism in modeling socio-technical systems, with each paradigm offering distinct methodological advantages and limitations.

title: Automatic Differentiation from Scratch: How PyTorch Computes Gradients in Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: 25 pages, 9 figures. Educational tutorial on automatic differentiation for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) using PyTorch. Includes complete numerical derivations and computational graph analysis
summary: This paper traces, with explicit numerical values, how PyTorch's automatic differentiation (AD) engine computes gradients for Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) training -- a setting that requires two levels of differentiation: computing the physics derivative $\hat{y}'(t)=d\hat{y}/dt$ through the network, and computing parameter gradients $\nabla_θL$ of a loss that itself depends on $\hat{y}'(t)$. Using a 1-3-3-1 multilayer perceptron and the initial value problem $y'(t)+y(t)=0$, $y(0)=1$, we trace the complete pipeline at every node: the computational graph built during the forward pass, the reverse-mode backward traversal that computes all 22 parameter gradients in a single pass, and the graph-on-graph mechanism by which \texttt{create\_graph=True} enables correct differentiation through the physics-informed residual. Every adjoint value is verified against the hand derivations of Tahimi (2026), connecting the $P/Q$ sensitivity framework to the vector--Jacobian products used by PyTorch's autograd engine.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a detailed numerical trace of how PyTorch's autograd computes gradients for PINNs, showing the two-level differentiation needed for physics-informed neural network training using a concrete example.
AI.motivation: To explicitly trace and understand how PyTorch's automatic differentiation engine computes gradients for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), which require two levels of differentiation: physics derivatives through the network and parameter gradients of a loss function that depends on those physics derivatives.
AI.method: Uses a 1-3-3-1 multilayer perceptron with the initial value problem y'(t)+y(t)=0, y(0)=1 to trace the complete computational pipeline at every node. This includes: the computational graph built during forward pass, reverse-mode backward traversal computing all 22 parameter gradients in one pass, and the graph-on-graph mechanism enabled by create_graph=True for correct differentiation through physics-informed residuals.
AI.result: Provides explicit numerical values for the complete gradient computation pipeline, verifying every adjoint value against hand derivations from Tahimi (2026), and connects the P/Q sensitivity framework to PyTorch's autograd vector-Jacobian products.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully traces and explains the complete automatic differentiation process for PINNs in PyTorch, demonstrating how graph-on-graph mechanisms enable correct second-order differentiation needed for physics-informed training, bridging the gap between theoretical sensitivity frameworks and practical autograd implementation.

title: OriginBlame: Record- and Token-Level Data Provenance for AI Training Datasets
summary: When a data contributor requests removal, model trainers face a practical gap: unlearning algorithms require a forget set, yet no tool can locate which training records belong to a given author. Existing provenance systems operate at file or dataset level, forcing catastrophic over-deletion. We present ob, a record- and token-level data provenance system that propagates author identity through data processing pipelines and resolves revocation requests into precise forget sets via deterministic queries. Evaluation on 219,555 Wikipedia pages demonstrates that record-level provenance eliminates dataset-level over-deletion (from 101x to 1.3x), while integration adds 1.3-4.0% throughput overhead (HuggingFace) and 2.1-19.0% (Datatrove) on wiki data. On a 1.7B model, provenance-based forget sets improve unlearning by 42% over random baselines.

AI.tldr: A record-level data provenance system called "ob" that enables precise identification of training records belonging to specific authors, allowing for targeted unlearning instead of catastrophic dataset-level deletion when handling data removal requests.
AI.motivation: When data contributors request removal of their data from models, there's a practical gap: unlearning algorithms need a specific "forget set" of training records to remove, but current tools can't identify which specific training records belong to which author. Existing provenance systems work only at file or dataset level, forcing model trainers to delete far more data than necessary.
AI.method: The paper presents "ob," a record- and token-level data provenance system. It propagates author identity through data processing pipelines and resolves revocation requests into precise forget sets via deterministic queries. The system was evaluated on 219,555 Wikipedia pages to measure effectiveness.
AI.result: Record-level provenance reduces over-deletion dramatically from 101x to 1.3x compared to dataset-level approaches. Integration adds modest overhead: 1.3-4.0% throughput overhead on HuggingFace and 2.1-19.0% on Datatrove for Wikipedia data. Provenance-based forget sets improve unlearning by 42% over random baselines on a 1.7B parameter model.
AI.conclusion: Record-level data provenance provides a practical solution to the unlearning gap by enabling precise identification of contributor-specific training data, dramatically reducing over-deletion while maintaining acceptable performance overhead, and significantly improving the effectiveness of machine unlearning algorithms.

title: Pezego-HITL: A policy-grounded large language model architecture for agricultural extension in Ghana
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in agricultural decision-support settings, yet high-stakes crop protection in smallholder agriculture requires more than output-quality benchmarks. Over a two-year design and evaluation programme, we formalise policy-constrained large language model assessment as an adaptive compute allocation problem that jointly captures safety compliance, helpfulness, operational latency, and expert supervision workload. We introduce P-EVAL (Policy-grounded Expert-calibrated VALidation protocol), a unified evaluation framework for policy-grounded decision support, evaluating the architecture on a simulated field query database consisting of 1,240 cases. The protocol is instantiated on the Pezego advisory architecture (Pezego-HITL) and evaluated in Ghana. Following offline judge calibration against gold-standard human expert decisions ($κ= 0.77$), we evaluate the architectural performance under simulated query workloads. Under P-EVAL, our memory-routed architecture improves the Policy Alignment Rate (PAR) to 0.94 and the Agronomic Utility Rate (AUR) to 0.95, while reducing P95 latency by 55% (from 28.6s to 12.9s) through a 59.6% cache reuse ratio. We also demonstrate generalisability using the open-source \texttt{Qwen3.5-9B-DeepSeek-V4-Flash} model, achieving a PAR of 0.86 and a 54.5% latency reduction (to 10.2s). To evaluate practical utility and socio-technical integration, we administer detailed questionnaires to Ghanaian Extension Services Officers ($N=30$) and smallholder farmers ($N=36$). Taken together, this work demonstrates how policy-grounded structured retrieval-augmented generation with validated-memory routing makes safety-utility-latency trade-offs explicit, offering a scalable template for trustworthy AI-driven extension in smallholder farming systems.

AI.tldr: P-EVAL framework evaluates LLM-based agricultural decision support with policy constraints, improving safety, utility, and latency through memory-routed architecture validated in Ghana.
AI.motivation: LLMs deployed in agricultural decision-support settings require more than output-quality benchmarks, especially for high-stakes crop protection in smallholder agriculture where safety, policy compliance, and practical utility are critical.
AI.method: Introduced P-EVAL (Policy-grounded Expert-calibrated VALidation protocol), a unified evaluation framework for policy-grounded decision support. Tested on Pezego-HITL architecture with memory routing, evaluated on 1,240 simulated field queries. Used judge calibration against gold-standard human expert decisions (κ=0.77) and tested both proprietary and open-source LLM models.
AI.result: Memory-routed architecture improved Policy Alignment Rate to 0.94 and Agronomic Utility Rate to 0.95, reduced P95 latency by 55% (28.6s to 12.9s) with 59.6% cache reuse. Open-source model achieved PAR 0.86 and 54.5% latency reduction. Questionnaires with Extension Services Officers (N=30) and farmers (N=36) showed practical utility and socio-technical integration.
AI.conclusion: Policy-grounded structured retrieval-augmented generation with validated-memory routing makes safety-utility-latency trade-offs explicit, offering a scalable template for trustworthy AI-driven extension in smallholder farming systems.

title: Beyond Backbone Backpropagation: A Decoupled Strategy for Efficient Transfer Learning
summary: Deep learning models achieve state-of-the-art image classification but face deployment challenges due to computational costs and energy demands. We propose a lightweight training strategy that adapts normalization layers of the model to the new domain and decouples feature extraction from classifier optimization, reducing overhead by precomputing features only once. A redesigned classifier head with margin-based weighted loss further minimizes ambiguity without end-to-end backpropagation. Evaluated across four CNN architectures (ResNet18, ResNet50, MobileNet, DenseNet121), three Transformer models (ViT, Swin and DeiT) and three medical datasets (Brain Cancer MRI, BreakHis and PatchCamelyon), our approach significantly reduces the required training time with only a marginal accuracy trade-off, often matching or surpassing baseline performance. This efficiency translates to reducing CO2 by orders of magnitude, offering a practical and environmentally sustainable solution for resource-constrained clinical or prototyping environments.

AI.tldr: Lightweight training strategy adapts normalization layers and decouples feature extraction from classifier optimization, achieving significant training time reduction and environmental benefits with minimal accuracy trade-off.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models face deployment challenges due to high computational costs and energy demands, especially problematic for resource-constrained clinical or prototyping environments where efficiency and sustainability are crucial.
AI.method: 1) Adapt normalization layers to new domain, 2) Decouple feature extraction from classifier optimization by precomputing features only once, 3) Redesign classifier head with margin-based weighted loss to minimize ambiguity without end-to-end backpropagation.
AI.result: Evaluated across four CNN architectures (ResNet18, ResNet50, MobileNet, DenseNet121) and three Transformer models (ViT, Swin, DeiT) on three medical datasets (Brain Cancer MRI, BreakHis, PatchCamelyon), showing significantly reduced training time with only marginal accuracy trade-off, often matching or surpassing baseline performance while reducing CO2 emissions by orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight training strategy offers a practical and environmentally sustainable solution for resource-constrained environments, achieving efficient deployment with minimal performance compromise.

title: SPINE: Bridging the Cyber-Physical Gap with Agentic AI
summary: Foundation models have given robots a sophisticated brain for complex decision-making, yet deploying that intelligence into a physical platform still demands tedious, expert-driven calibration. This deployment gap, the robot's spinal cord, remains a primary bottleneck to scalable Embodied AI. Hence, we propose SPINE (Scalable Physical Integration with ageNtic Expertise): an agentic framework for systematically debugging and deploying bimanual robots with minimal robotics expertise. SPINE's harness comprises two orchestrated multi-agent workflows: a profile builder that creates robot-specific context, and a debugger that cycles through diagnosis, repair, and validation until teleoperation works. Across seven DOBOT X-Trainer debugging scenarios, a robotics novice using SPINE outperformed human operators using Claude Code with the same reference materials, but without SPINE's structured workflow, improving operationalization success from 75% to 100% and reducing mean time-to-teleoperation from 16 min 45 s to 13 min 47 s. On AgileX PiPER, a distinct ROS/CAN bimanual arm, SPINE resolved all 10 implanted bugs, versus 9 out of 10 for the expert baseline, in nearly the same amount of time. Together, these results show that SPINE can transfer across bimanual platforms, reduce dependence on expert calibration, and move embodied AI closer to scalable real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: SPINE is an agentic framework that enables systematic debugging and deployment of bimanual robots with minimal robotics expertise, bridging the gap between AI intelligence and physical robot calibration.
AI.motivation: While foundation models provide robots with sophisticated decision-making capabilities, deploying this intelligence into physical platforms requires tedious, expert-driven calibration. This "deployment gap" (referred to as the robot's spinal cord) remains a primary bottleneck to scalable Embodied AI.
AI.method: SPINE comprises two orchestrated multi-agent workflows: 1) a profile builder that creates robot-specific context, and 2) a debugger that cycles through diagnosis, repair, and validation until teleoperation works properly.
AI.result: In DOBOT X-Trainer debugging scenarios, SPINE improved operationalization success from 75% to 100% and reduced mean time-to-teleoperation from 16:45 to 13:47 compared to baseline. On AgileX PiPER bimanual arm, SPINE resolved all 10 implanted bugs versus 9/10 for expert baseline in similar time.
AI.conclusion: SPINE demonstrates transferability across bimanual platforms, reduces dependence on expert calibration, and moves embodied AI closer to scalable real-world deployment by systematically bridging the gap between AI intelligence and physical robot implementation.

title: Federated Explainable Artificial Intelligence: Roles, Architectures, Evaluation, and Open Challenges
summary: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a key paradigm for privacy-preserving collaborative model training across distributed and heterogeneous data sources. By keeping raw data local, FL addresses data confidentiality concerns, yet it does not resolve the opacity of modern machine learning models. In parallel, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) has gained attention for improving transparency, trust, and accountability, particularly in high-stakes domains. Their intersection has given rise to Federated Explainable Artificial Intelligence (FedXAI) paradigm, which aims to jointly satisfy privacy and explainability requirements. This survey provides a systematic review of FedXAI, highlighting the transition of explainability from a post-hoc tool to an integral component of the FL lifecycle. We show how explainability supports aggregation, personalization, robustness, coordination, and system-level decision making. To organize the literature, we introduce a taxonomy that classifies FedXAI methods by the role of explainability, model and explainer types, explanation scope, integration level, FL settings, and data heterogeneity. We review approaches ranging from model-agnostic explanations to interpretable federated models and explainability-aware aggregation mechanisms. We also examine evaluation practices and discuss the lack of standardized benchmarks and metrics for measuring explanation quality, stability, privacy leakage, and computational overhead. Finally, we identify key challenges, including explainability under non-IID data, explanation-centric security threats, communication-efficient XAI, continual FedXAI, and the integration of domain knowledge and regulatory constraints. By consolidating existing work and identifying key gaps, this survey serves as a reference framework for designing trustworthy, transparent, and privacy-preserving federated AI systems.

AI.tldr: This survey paper reviews Federated Explainable AI (FedXAI) - the intersection of Federated Learning and Explainable AI - focusing on how explainability can be integrated throughout the FL lifecycle to jointly satisfy privacy and transparency requirements.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning addresses data privacy by keeping raw data local, but doesn't solve model opacity issues. Explainable AI improves transparency in high-stakes domains. Their convergence creates a need for systems that satisfy both privacy and explainability requirements, leading to the FedXAI paradigm.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic review and develop a comprehensive taxonomy classifying FedXAI methods by: role of explainability, model/explainer types, explanation scope, integration level, FL settings, and data heterogeneity. They review approaches from model-agnostic explanations to interpretable federated models and explainability-aware aggregation mechanisms.
AI.result: The survey provides a structured framework showing how explainability supports FL operations including aggregation, personalization, robustness, coordination, and system-level decision making. It identifies current gaps in standardized benchmarks and evaluation metrics for explanation quality, stability, privacy leakage, and computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: FedXAI represents a crucial development for trustworthy, transparent, and privacy-preserving AI systems. The survey consolidates existing work and identifies key research challenges including explainability under non-IID data, explanation-centric security threats, communication-efficient XAI, continual FedXAI, and integration of domain knowledge with regulatory constraints.

title: Interventional Grounding Audits: Black-Box Premise-Dependency Tests for LLM Chain-of-Thought via Predicate Substitution
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models (https://iclr.cc/virtual/2026/10017466)
summary: Large language models produce chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning that appears logically sound yet may not genuinely depend on its stated premises. We introduce interventional grounding audits, a black-box, step-level test of premise dependency: we intervene on a single premise by substituting its target predicate with a fresh symbol, re-run the model, and check whether each reasoning step's normalized conclusion (canonical predicate form) changes. We evaluate on ProntoQA, a synthetic multi-hop deductive reasoning benchmark with gold proof trees, where step-level premise dependencies are known. Applied to 50 ProntoQA problems with GPT-4o, our method achieves F1 = 0.806 on detecting proof-tree dependencies (F1 = 0.885 on predicate-determining dependencies; Recall = 100%), significantly outperforming a self-consistency baseline (F1 = 0.343; 95% bootstrap CIs non-overlapping). We further identify that 66% of correctly-solved problems contain at least one aligned step insensitive to a direct proof-tree dependency under consistent substitution -- all involving entity-introduction premises, a documented blind spot of the consistent-substitution evaluator -- a "right answer, wrong reasoning" signal invisible to passive methods. All audit certificates, raw outputs, and reproduction scripts are available in a public GitHub repository, and we discuss scope limits beyond formal, parsable benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces interventional grounding audits to test whether LLMs' chain-of-thought reasoning genuinely depends on stated premises by substituting predicates with fresh symbols and checking if reasoning conclusions change.
AI.motivation: LLMs produce chain-of-thought reasoning that appears logical but may not genuinely depend on stated premises, creating a need for methods to audit premise dependency in reasoning.
AI.method: Introduces interventional grounding audits: black-box, step-level test where single premises are intervened by substituting target predicates with fresh symbols, then models are re-run to check if normalized conclusion changes.
AI.result: On ProntoQA benchmark with GPT-4o, method achieves F1=0.806 on detecting proof-tree dependencies, significantly outperforming self-consistency baseline (F1=0.343). Identifies 66% of correctly-solved problems contain aligned but insensitive steps to direct dependencies.
AI.conclusion: Method effectively detects "right answer, wrong reasoning" patterns invisible to passive methods, particularly exposing issues with entity-introduction premises, though scope limited to formal, parsable benchmarks.

title: What Your Model Threw Away and Why You'll Want It Back: Masking, Fingerprinting, and Privacy from Discarded Geometry
summary: We develop a framework for the information discarded by machine learning models whose inputs carry a Lie group action. Given a representation $π$ of a Lie group $G$ on a space $V$ and a learned function $f\colon V \to \mathbb{R}$, we define two objects measuring the symmetry invisible to $f$. The null fiber at a point $x \in V$ is the set $N_G(f,x) = \{g \in G : f(π(g^{-1}) \cdot x) = f(x)\}$ of group elements whose inverse action on $x$ is undetectable by $f$. When $N_G(f,x)$ is independent of $x$, it coincides with the stabilizer $\mathrm{Stab}_G(f)$, the largest subgroup of $G$ under which $f$ is invariant. For smooth maps to $\mathbb{R}$, the preimage theorem guarantees that null fibers have dimension at least $\dim G - 1$ at generic inputs, regardless of architecture. For compact groups acting on themselves, the Peter--Weyl theorem yields a spectral characterization of both objects in terms of the Fourier coefficient matrices of $f$. We show that null fiber elements can be computed efficiently via Newton iteration on the orbit map, at a cost comparable to a few gradient evaluations. Applications to data masking, model fingerprinting, and privacy-preserving computation are developed and tested experimentally on molecular property prediction under $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ and spherical image classification under the Möbius group $\mathrm{PSL}(2, \mathbb{C})$. The framework applies uniformly to classical neural networks and variational quantum circuits.

AI.tldr: A framework to quantify symmetry information discarded by ML models with Lie group actions, defining null fibers and stabilizers to measure undetectable symmetries, with applications in data privacy and model analysis.
AI.motivation: Machine learning models often ignore or discard symmetry information present in data with group structures (like rotations, translations). Understanding what symmetry information is lost helps improve model interpretability, privacy, and data masking capabilities.
AI.method: Defines null fibers N_G(f,x) (group elements undetectable by f at input x) and stabilizers Stab_G(f) (global symmetries of f). Uses preimage theorem for smooth maps, Peter-Weyl theorem for compact groups, and computes null fibers via Newton iteration on orbit maps.
AI.result: Shows null fibers have dimension at least dim G - 1 for generic inputs, provides spectral characterization via Fourier coefficients for compact groups, demonstrates efficient computation via Newton iteration (cost comparable to few gradient evaluations), and validates applications on SO(3) molecular property prediction and PSL(2,C) spherical image classification.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides systematic tools to measure discarded symmetry information in ML models, enabling applications in data masking, model fingerprinting, and privacy-preserving computation across classical neural networks and quantum circuits.

title: Probabilistic Extension of Neuro-Symbolic AGI Robots based on Belnap's Typed Intensional FOL
summary: Neuro-symbolic AI based on $IFOL_B$ is a way to combine neural learning and symbolic reasoning to overcome limitations of purely neural systems (like lack of interpretability and logical structure) with formal logical machinery for self-reference. In this paper we expand the cognitive power of $IFOL_B$ by using the probability computation for the currently unknown sentences, based on Nilsson's probability structure for the $IFOL_B$. We introduce the global symmetry transformation that preserves the current knowledge database and logical deduction, and the local one used for real-time decisions about concrete (sub)problems that involve only a very strict subset of $IFOL_B$ predicates. The computation of probability density function $KI$ in both cases, based on the Shannon's maximum information entropy, is provided by neural networks of this probabilistic neuro-symbolic AGI.

AI.tldr: This paper extends neuro-symbolic AI based on $IFOL_B$ by introducing probability computation for unknown sentences using Nilsson's probability structure, with global and local symmetry transformations for knowledge preservation and real-time decisions.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of purely neural systems (lack of interpretability and logical structure) by combining neural learning with symbolic reasoning using $IFOL_B$, and to expand cognitive power by handling uncertainty through probability computation for unknown sentences.
AI.method: Introduces probability computation based on Nilsson's probability structure for $IFOL_B$, with global symmetry transformations that preserve knowledge databases and logical deduction, and local transformations for real-time decisions on strict subsets of $IFOL_B$ predicates. Uses Shannon's maximum information entropy to compute probability density function $KI$ via neural networks.
AI.result: Developed a probabilistic neuro-symbolic AGI framework that combines neural networks with symbolic reasoning in $IFOL_B$, enabling uncertainty handling through probability computations for unknown sentences while maintaining logical consistency through symmetry transformations.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully extends neuro-symbolic AI based on $IFOL_B$ to handle uncertainty probabilistically, creating a more powerful cognitive architecture that maintains interpretability while addressing unknown situations through neural network-based probability computations.

title: Targeted Recovery of Weight-Space Mechanisms From Neural Networks
comment: Accepted at the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop, ICML 2026
summary: Parameter decomposition (PD) decomposes neural networks into interpretable computational components that faithfully reflect the original network's operations. However, scaling PD to large models requires vast compute, making it a costly and risky endeavor. Here we propose targeted PD (tPD), which identifies only the components that process specific inputs of interest -- from isolated prompts to large subtasks -- by introducing a high-rank catch-all component that handles all non-target data. We validate tPD on toy models and on transformer language models trained on The Pile, where it recovers reproducible, mechanistically faithful circuits. We extract a CSS-only submodel of a 4-block transformer using 7% of the FLOPs of its published decomposition, and in a 12-block transformer we surgically ablate and rewire memorized sequences, with negligible side effects on other inputs.

AI.tldr: Targeted Parameter Decomposition (tPD) enables efficient extraction of interpretable computational components for specific inputs by using a high-rank catch-all component, reducing computational costs from full model decomposition.
AI.motivation: Standard Parameter Decomposition (PD) for neural networks is computationally expensive and risky when scaling to large models, requiring vast compute resources to decompose entire networks.
AI.method: Targeted PD (tPD) identifies only components that process specific inputs of interest (from isolated prompts to large subtasks) by introducing a high-rank catch-all component that handles all non-target data, avoiding full model decomposition.
AI.result: tPD successfully recovers reproducible, mechanistically faithful circuits in transformer language models; extracts CSS-only submodels with 7% FLOPs of full decomposition; and enables surgical ablation/rewiring of memorized sequences with negligible side effects.
AI.conclusion: tPD provides an efficient, scalable approach for interpretable circuit extraction from large neural networks, making mechanistic interpretability more practical by targeting specific input processing components instead of full model decomposition.

title: Self-Improvements in Modern Agentic Systems: A Survey
comment: 97 pages, 12 figures. Project page: https://selfimproving-agent.github.io/ Repository: https://github.com/selfimproving-agent/awesome-Self-Improving-Agents
summary: Self-improving autonomous agents are moving from research prototypes to deployed systems. The primary goal is controllable evolution, or adaptation, from experience with minimal or even no human input. This survey frames modern self-improving agents as adaptive systems that convert experience into accumulated capability gains. We offer a system-level framework that represents a modern agent as a configuration coupling a foundation model with an operational scaffold of prompts, memory, tools, and control logic. Within this framework, self-improvement is formalized as a self-induced update operator that obtains and commits updates to model parameters or scaffold components. We organize prior work by update target and by the signals that drive change, then review applications and discuss evaluation, before closing with open problems and future directions. For convenience, we track technical updates on https://github.com/selfimproving-agent/awesome-Self-Improving-Agents.

AI.tldr: A survey paper presenting a system-level framework for self-improving autonomous agents that formalizes how agents can accumulate capability gains through experience-driven updates to model parameters and scaffold components.
AI.motivation: Self-improving autonomous agents are transitioning from research prototypes to deployed systems, requiring formal frameworks for controllable evolution from experience with minimal human input. There's a need to systematically understand how agents can convert experience into accumulated capability gains.
AI.method: The authors propose a system-level framework representing modern agents as configurations coupling foundation models with operational scaffolds (prompts, memory, tools, control logic). Self-improvement is formalized as a self-induced update operator that obtains and commits updates to model parameters or scaffold components.
AI.result: A comprehensive survey organizing prior work by update target and signals driving change, with review of applications and discussion of evaluation methods. The framework provides systematic understanding of self-improving agent architectures and their evolutionary mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a foundational framework for understanding self-improving agents, identifies open problems and future directions in the field, and provides practical resources for ongoing technical updates through a dedicated GitHub repository.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Sequential Decision Rules for Event-Triggered LLM Invocation in Streaming Systems
comment: 18 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to the ECML PKDD 2026 Research Track
summary: Streaming inference pipelines increasingly pair lightweight fast models with Large Language Models (LLMs) that provide rich semantic understanding at substantial cost. The central question of when to invoke the LLM has received limited formal treatment. We cast this as a risk-based sequential stopping problem, where a trigger policy fires when a risk functional over the observation history exceeds a threshold. Within this framework, we prove six results: a minimum inter-event time bound excluding trigger chattering; optimality of threshold policies via smooth pasting; approximate SPRT guarantees under estimated parameters; O(sqrt(T log T)) regret for stationary streams, extending to O(sqrt((C_T + 1) T log T)) under C_T changepoints; O(1/sqrt(T)) convergence of online gradient descent for adaptive thresholds; and a calibration-to-miss-rate transfer inequality. Several classical trigger families, including event-triggered, optimal stopping, SPRT, CUSUM, and Bayesian triggers, can be expressed as special cases of this framework. On turbofan degradation data (CMAPSS) with real LLM calls, we empirically verify the theoretical assumptions, ablate the risk function design, compare against six baselines including a RouteLLM-style router and contextual bandits, and analyze cost sensitivity and LLM failure modes. The results confirm sublinear regret, with alpha < 1 for all principled triggers; high diagnostic quality, with 92.9 percent of 1600 LLM diagnoses reaching grounding score >= 0.75 under our rubric; and that anomaly-score-driven risk functions dominate alternatives by roughly an order of magnitude on the Pareto AUC.

AI.tldr: Streaming inference framework pairs fast models with expensive LLMs, using risk-based sequential stopping to decide when to invoke LLMs, achieving sublinear regret and high diagnostic quality.
AI.motivation: Streaming inference pipelines increasingly combine lightweight fast models with expensive LLMs, but there's limited formal treatment of when to invoke LLMs. The paper aims to provide a principled framework for deciding when to trigger LLM calls based on risk assessment.
AI.method: Cast the problem as a risk-based sequential stopping problem where a trigger policy fires when a risk functional over observation history exceeds a threshold. Framework includes six theoretical results covering inter-event time bounds, optimal threshold policies, SPRT guarantees, regret bounds, online gradient descent convergence, and calibration-miss-rate transfer inequality.
AI.result: On turbofan degradation data (CMAPSS) with real LLM calls: confirms sublinear regret (alpha < 1 for all principled triggers), high diagnostic quality (92.9% of 1600 LLM diagnoses reach grounding score >= 0.75), and anomaly-score-driven risk functions dominate alternatives by roughly an order of magnitude on Pareto AUC.
AI.conclusion: The proposed risk-based sequential stopping framework provides a principled, theoretically-grounded approach for deciding when to invoke expensive LLMs in streaming inference pipelines, with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical performance that outperforms multiple baselines.

title: Improving Molecular Property Prediction in Small Language Models Using Graph-based Tools
comment: Presented at the 2nd Causal Neuro-symbolic Artificial Intelligence (Causal NeSy): Toward Agentic LLMs with Neuro-Symbolic and Graph Based Reasoning Workshop @ ESWC2026
summary: Small language models (SLMs) have shown promise for zero-shot molecular property prediction from SMILES strings, yet they often suffer from structural blindness because sequence representations under-specify key graph-topological cues. We propose a modular Context-Augmented Prompting framework that enables agentic tool use at inference time: a trained GNN expert model provides a predictive hint with confidence, and a GNN extracts an instance-specific explanatory subgraph (e.g., a subgraph SMILES and an accompanying explanatory paragraph). We evaluate three commonly used SLMs on MUTAG and Tox21 under five prompting configurations ranging from SMILES-only to using all available tools at hand. Across two datasets, enriching prompts with graph-derived context yields substantial accuracy gains, often exceeding 25% relative improvement and up to 74% on Tox21. We further validate the functional relevance of the extracted motifs via a necessity-based edge-drop intervention. Despite the observed gains, a persistent gap remains to specialized GNN models, highlighting both the value and limits of text-conditioned reasoning for molecular structure.

AI.tldr: SLMs enhanced with GNN-derived context show significant gains in molecular property prediction but still lag behind specialized GNNs.
AI.motivation: Small language models struggle with structural blindness in molecular property prediction from SMILES strings because sequence representations miss key graph-topological information.
AI.method: Proposed a modular Context-Augmented Prompting framework that enables agentic tool use at inference: a GNN expert provides predictive hints with confidence, and another GNN extracts explanatory subgraphs (subgraph SMILES + explanatory paragraph).
AI.result: Graph-derived context yields substantial accuracy gains (often >25% relative improvement, up to 74% on Tox21), validated via necessity-based edge-drop interventions, but a persistent gap remains to specialized GNN models.
AI.conclusion: While enriching prompts with graph-derived context significantly improves SLM performance for molecular property prediction, text-conditioned reasoning has inherent limits compared to specialized graph models.

title: TSSM: Triaxial State Space Model for Global Station Weather Forecasting with Temporal-Variable-Historical Modeling
summary: Global Station Weather Forecasting (GSWF) is pivotal for localized and extreme weather prediction over key regions. Despite efforts to exploit look-back windows, existing methods show limited accuracy gains and struggle with extreme events and error accumulation. These limitations stem from overreliance on short-term patterns, which are insufficient to capture chaotic weather dynamics, especially under partial observations. To address this problem, we propose a novel Triaxial State Space Model (TSSM) with a history-enhanced Temporal-VariableHistorical paradigm, which incorporates period-aligned historical weather data to compensate for long-term, large-scale periodic, and full-window weather patterns beyond the temporal lookback window. Specifically, TSSM stacks historical samples into period-aligned batches, where forecasting is causally supported by historical and current observations. Temporal, variable, and historical scanning are designed to capture axial temporal dependencies, variable correlations, and historical evolution. This structure is hierarchically shared to model seasonal to extreme events while alleviating misalignment across historical patterns. TSSM achieves SOTA performance on Weather-5K, the largest station weather dataset to date, with 10% and 61% gains in accuracy and extreme event metrics, and obtains 95% best or second-best results on human-involved datasets. Its advantages are more pronounced in long-horizon and iterative forecasting, reaching a 37.5% gain at 240h and up to 103.5% under a 48h times 5 iterative setting. Moreover, TSSM retains > 90% performance under up to 80% missing observations, compared with < 43% for baselines, demonstrating robustness and practical potential for reliable GSWF in global in-situ observation networks.

AI.tldr: TSSM improves global station weather forecasting by incorporating period-aligned historical data to capture long-term patterns, achieving SOTA performance with robustness to missing observations.
AI.motivation: Existing GSWF methods have limited accuracy, struggle with extreme events and error accumulation due to overreliance on short-term patterns insufficient for chaotic weather dynamics, especially under partial observations.
AI.method: Proposes Triaxial State Space Model (TSSM) with history-enhanced Temporal-Variable-Historical paradigm that stacks period-aligned historical weather data batches to support forecasting causally with historical and current observations, using temporal, variable, and historical scanning to capture axial dependencies.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA on Weather-5K (largest station dataset) with 10% accuracy gain and 61% extreme event metric improvement; 95% best/second-best results on human-involved datasets; 37.5% gain at 240h long-horizon forecasting; up to 103.5% improvement in iterative forecasting; >90% performance retention under 80% missing observations vs <43% for baselines.
AI.conclusion: TSSM effectively addresses limitations of existing methods by incorporating historical patterns, demonstrating superior accuracy, extreme event prediction, robustness to missing data, and practical potential for reliable GSWF in global observation networks.

title: Oracle Agent Memory as an Enterprise Memory Substrate for Long-Horizon AI Agents
comment: 23 pages, 7 figures. Technical report on Oracle Agent Memory
summary: Agent memory is a systems problem for long-horizon agents. Practical deployments require retention of task state across extended conversations, recovery of user-specific facts and preferences across sessions, and accumulation of procedural knowledge from prior outcomes. These requirements extend beyond document retrieval: a memory layer must determine which interactions become durable state, how that state is scoped, how it is retrieved under latency constraints, and how it is revised or removed over time. This report studies Oracle Agent Memory as a database-native memory substrate built on Oracle Database. Three themes organize the discussion: memory as a lifecycle spanning ingestion, extraction, consolidation, retrieval, summarization, and revision or removal; a layered architecture that separates an active memory core from a passive memory-store interface with explicit scope control across users, agents, and threads; and evaluation methodology in which downstream task accuracy is complemented by memory-centric measures such as evidence retrieval, recall, latency, and estimated token use. The report summarizes LongMemEval results, reaching 93.8% accuracy, compares Oracle Agent Memory against flat-history baselines, using about 10.7x fewer tokens, and published or reported external baselines where available, and closes with implementation-oriented appendix material covering setup, thread lifecycle, and search semantics.

AI.tldr: Oracle Agent Memory is a database-native memory system for AI agents that manages memory lifecycle, uses layered architecture, and achieves high accuracy with reduced token usage compared to flat-history approaches.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents lack proper memory systems for long-horizon tasks. They need to retain task state across extended conversations, recover user-specific facts across sessions, and accumulate procedural knowledge, going beyond simple document retrieval. A proper memory layer must handle memory lifecycle, scoping, retrieval under latency constraints, and revision/removal over time.
AI.method: Oracle Agent Memory is built on Oracle Database with three key themes: 1) Memory as a lifecycle spanning ingestion, extraction, consolidation, retrieval, summarization, and revision/removal; 2) Layered architecture separating active memory core from passive memory-store interface with explicit scope control across users, agents, and threads; 3) Evaluation methodology complementing task accuracy with memory-centric measures like evidence retrieval, recall, latency, and token usage.
AI.result: The system achieves 93.8% accuracy on LongMemEval, uses about 10.7x fewer tokens than flat-history baselines, and shows strong performance against available external baselines. Implementation includes practical considerations for setup, thread lifecycle, and search semantics.
AI.conclusion: Oracle Agent Memory provides a practical, database-native solution for agent memory systems problems, demonstrating significant efficiency gains and high accuracy while addressing the full memory lifecycle needed for real-world long-horizon AI agent deployments.

title: Disentangling Knowledge States with Ability and Proficiency Modeling for Knowledge Tracing
summary: Knowledge tracing (KT) aims to predict students' future performance by modeling their evolving knowledge states from historical interactions. Existing KT methods usually treat the raw interaction sequence as a unified behavioral process, overlooking the phase-specific nature of learning behaviors. Our preliminary observations show that students are more likely to correctly answer previously failed knowledge concepts after sufficient practice, suggesting a transition from ability-building to proficiency-oriented learning. Motivated by this, we propose Phase-Aware Knowledge Tracing (PAKT), a KT framework that decomposes student interactions into ability and proficiency phases based on the tailored decomposition mechanism. To effectively exploit the decomposed sequences, we design a multi-branch Transformer with a type-aware readout module to jointly capture phase-specific and holistic knowledge states. We further provide a causal analysis to reveal the confounding bias caused by entangling complex learning behaviors in phase-agnostic KT models. Extensive experiments on six public benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms representative baselines, with a maximum AUC gain of 1.33% and an average gain of 0.82%.

AI.tldr: PAKT introduces a phase-aware knowledge tracing framework that decomposes learning into ability and proficiency phases, using multi-branch Transformers to capture phase-specific knowledge states and outperform existing KT methods.
AI.motivation: Existing KT methods treat interaction sequences as unified behavioral processes, overlooking the phase-specific nature of learning behaviors. Observations show students are more likely to correctly answer previously failed concepts after sufficient practice, suggesting a transition from ability-building to proficiency-oriented learning.
AI.method: Proposes Phase-Aware Knowledge Tracing (PAKT) framework that decomposes student interactions into ability and proficiency phases using tailored decomposition mechanism. Uses multi-branch Transformer with type-aware readout module to jointly capture phase-specific and holistic knowledge states. Provides causal analysis to reveal confounding bias in phase-agnostic models.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six public benchmarks show consistent outperformance over representative baselines, with maximum AUC gain of 1.33% and average gain of 0.82%.
AI.conclusion: PAKT effectively captures phase-specific learning behaviors, addressing limitations of existing phase-agnostic KT models and demonstrating superior performance through decomposition of ability and proficiency phases.

title: Learning Safe Agent Behaviour from Human Preferences and Justifications via World Models
comment: 42 pages, 18 figures. Extended version of a paper presented at ICAART 2026; submitted for consideration in the ICAART 2026 post-publication selected-papers volume in Lecture Notes in Artificial Intelligence
summary: We address the problem of safely training an agent policy and deploying a good and safe policy, in settings where the environment dynamics are unknown and no suitable reward function is available. In the context of safety-critical environments, we consider traditional reinforcement learning impractical and resort to the resource of human input. We introduce DROPJ, a human-centred method for both safe training and deployment. We first learn a world model (a learned simulator) from a dataset of prior real-world trajectories. A human then plays the game in this learned simulator to extract several informative simulated trajectories. From these, we sample pairs of simulated trajectory segments and elicit from a human their preference over these segments, as well as a reason (justification) for their choice. We then train a reward model from these justified preferences and use it, together with the world model, to directly deploy the agent using model predictive control. Running real-user experiments, we find that generating informative simulated trajectories from a user significantly reduces the computational cost during training compared to other strategies, and can also improve the performance during deployment. In the context of training within a learned simulator, we show that the use of preferences rather than other types of feedback substantially improves the performance during deployment. We further demonstrate that safety justifications accompanying preferences can significantly enhance safety or prioritise user-prescribed aspects of safety associated with them during deployment.

AI.tldr: DROPJ is a human-centered method that uses a learned world model, human gameplay in simulation, justified preferences, and model predictive control to safely train and deploy agents in unknown, safety-critical environments without explicit rewards.
AI.motivation: In safety-critical environments with unknown dynamics and no suitable reward function, traditional reinforcement learning is impractical. There's a need for safe training and deployment methods that leverage human input when explicit rewards are unavailable.
AI.method: 1) Learn a world model from prior real-world trajectories; 2) Have humans play in the learned simulator to generate informative trajectories; 3) Sample trajectory segment pairs and collect human preferences with justifications; 4) Train a reward model from justified preferences; 5) Use the reward model and world model with model predictive control for direct deployment.
AI.result: Generating informative simulated trajectories reduces computational training costs and improves deployment performance. Using preferences rather than other feedback types substantially improves deployment performance. Safety justifications accompanying preferences significantly enhance safety or prioritize user-prescribed safety aspects during deployment.
AI.conclusion: DROPJ successfully addresses safe training and deployment in unknown, safety-critical environments by combining learned world models, human-generated simulations, justified preferences, and model predictive control, demonstrating improved efficiency, performance, and safety prioritization.

title: STKAN: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks for Spatio-Temporal Forecasting
summary: Real-world traffic data exhibit heterogeneous spatial correlations and nonlinear temporal dynamics, posing substantial challenges for accurate spatio-temporal forecasting. Existing approaches have developed increasingly sophisticated graph, attention, and decomposition architectures, while the influence of the underlying nonlinear function approximator has received comparatively less attention. In this work, we propose STKAN, a spatio-temporal forecasting architecture that introduces Taylor-polynomial Kolmogorov--Arnold Network modules into spatial and temporal token mixing. STKAN first constructs high-level spatial representations through a learnable soft node-group assignment mechanism, applies group-wise spatial mixing, and subsequently models temporal dependencies over the compressed sequence. Spatial and temporal self-attention layers are further employed to capture long-range interactions. Experiments on five traffic forecasting benchmarks show that STKAN achieves competitive performance and performs better than the evaluated MLP-based variant in the tested settings. These results suggest that the design of nonlinear function approximators can serve as a useful complement to architectural design in spatio-temporal forecasting.

AI.tldr: STKAN introduces Taylor-polynomial Kolmogorov-Arnold Network modules into spatial and temporal token mixing for traffic forecasting, achieving competitive performance by focusing on nonlinear function approximators as a complement to architectural design.
AI.motivation: Real-world traffic data exhibits heterogeneous spatial correlations and nonlinear temporal dynamics that are challenging to model. Existing approaches focus on graph, attention, and decomposition architectures, while the influence of nonlinear function approximators has received less attention.
AI.method: STKAN introduces Taylor-polynomial Kolmogorov-Arnold Network modules into spatial and temporal token mixing. It constructs high-level spatial representations through learnable soft node-group assignment, applies group-wise spatial mixing, models temporal dependencies over compressed sequences, and uses spatial/temporal self-attention for long-range interactions.
AI.result: Experiments on five traffic forecasting benchmarks show STKAN achieves competitive performance and outperforms the evaluated MLP-based variant in tested settings.
AI.conclusion: The design of nonlinear function approximators can serve as a useful complement to architectural design in spatio-temporal forecasting, as demonstrated by STKAN's success.

title: CayleyR: Solving the TopSpin puzzle via cycle intersection
summary: We present cayleyR, an R package for solving permutation puzzles by detecting cycle intersections in Cayley graphs. The core algorithm performs an iterative bidirectional search: from both the initial and target permutation states, random operation sequences generate cycles in the Cayley graph of the symmetric group Sn; their intersection yields a connecting path. When no direct intersection is found, a distance-guided bridge selection narrows the gap, and the process repeats. The package targets the TopSpin(n,k) puzzle, whose state space is a Cayley graph of Sn generated by a cyclic shift and a prefix reversal. We describe the mathematical framework, the algorithm, and its implementation, which combines a C++ hash-indexed state store with optional Vulkan GPU acceleration. The software is publicly available on CRAN.

AI.tldr: cayleyR is an R package that solves permutation puzzles like TopSpin by detecting cycle intersections in Cayley graphs through iterative bidirectional search with optional GPU acceleration.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of solving permutation puzzles, specifically the TopSpin puzzle, which has a state space represented as a Cayley graph of the symmetric group Sn. There's a need for efficient algorithms to find solution paths in these complex combinatorial spaces.
AI.method: The core algorithm performs iterative bidirectional search: from both initial and target permutation states, random operation sequences generate cycles in the Cayley graph. The method detects cycle intersections to find connecting paths. When no direct intersection is found, it uses distance-guided bridge selection to narrow the gap and repeats the process. The implementation combines a C++ hash-indexed state store with optional Vulkan GPU acceleration.
AI.result: The paper presents the cayleyR R package that successfully implements the algorithm for solving TopSpin(n,k) puzzles. The package is publicly available on CRAN and provides efficient permutation puzzle solving through the described mathematical framework and implementation approach.
AI.conclusion: The cayleyR package offers an effective solution for permutation puzzle solving by leveraging Cayley graph theory and cycle intersection detection, with practical implementation benefits including GPU acceleration for performance enhancement.

title: A Hybrid Mamba for Audio-Visual Navigation
comment: Main paper (6 pages). Accepted for publication by IEEE International Conference on Systems and Man and Cybernetics 2026 (IEEE SMC 2026)
summary: Since the paradigm centered on convolutional neural networks and recurrent architectures was established in 2020, the fundamental backbone networks for audio-visual navigation have undergone no essential changes for more than five years, making them inadequate to support efficient representation of dynamic multimodal sequences. This paper proposes Samba(A Hybrid Mamba for Audio-Visual Navigation). It uses the adaptive selection-enabled Mamba State Encoder (M-SE) to replace conventional GRUs for temporal aggregation, and constructs an Audio Mamba Encoder (AME) to remedy the limitations of convolutional operators in capturing global time-frequency dependencies in spectrograms. Experiments demonstrate that Samba exhibits exceptional generalization performance when facing unheard sound sources and unseen scenes. On the Matterport3D dataset, it improves the navigation success rate (SR) by 11.3\% compared with existing state-of-the-art models, and the performance gain is even more pronounced on the Replica dataset, which features finer scene structures. Such modernized architectural reconstruction unlocks stronger embodied representation capabilities at a lower computational cost, thereby providing a highly robust technical pathway for paradigm evolution in the field of audio-visual navigation.

AI.tldr: Samba (Hybrid Mamba for Audio-Visual Navigation) replaces conventional GRUs with Mamba-based temporal aggregation and introduces Audio Mamba Encoder to capture global time-frequency dependencies, achieving significant performance gains with lower computational cost.
AI.motivation: The fundamental backbone networks for audio-visual navigation have remained unchanged for over five years since 2020, making them inadequate for efficient representation of dynamic multimodal sequences. Current architectures based on convolutional neural networks and recurrent architectures cannot effectively capture global dependencies in audio spectrograms.
AI.method: Proposes Samba with two key innovations: 1) Adaptive selection-enabled Mamba State Encoder (M-SE) to replace conventional GRUs for temporal aggregation, and 2) Audio Mamba Encoder (AME) to address limitations of convolutional operators in capturing global time-frequency dependencies in spectrograms.
AI.result: Samba demonstrates exceptional generalization to unheard sound sources and unseen scenes. On Matterport3D dataset, improves navigation success rate by 11.3% over state-of-the-art models. On Replica dataset with finer scene structures, performance gains are even more pronounced. Achieves stronger representation capabilities at lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Samba's modernized architectural reconstruction unlocks stronger embodied representation capabilities with lower computational requirements, providing a highly robust technical pathway for paradigm evolution in audio-visual navigation field.

title: Networked Intelligence: Active Shared Context Graphs for Human-AI Team Science
summary: Most AI-for-science systems focus on scaling a single reasoning process through better models, larger context windows, long-horizon agentic execution, or digital co-scientists working with one principal user. However, challenging scientific problems are rarely solved by one reasoner alone. They are solved by teams whose members bring different priors, experimental backgrounds, tacit knowledge, and domain-trained intuitions. The open problem is therefore not only how to scale models, but how to cultivate networked intelligence: scaling the connections between humans and AI systems so that a result or hypothesis produced in one context reaches another person, agent, instrument, or robot that can act on it. We introduce Mycelium, an active shared workspace that automatically connects researchers and AI agents as a multi-user co-scientist. As human users and agents work, the system captures important observations and hypotheses, tracks how they relate to the team's evolving model, and routes them to the person or agent whose next decision they can inform. We evaluate Mycelium in its first empirical test, a biological multi-omics campaign in which routed shared context turned a local analytical finding into a cross-expert mechanistic constraint and ultimately into an experimental design. We also give networked intelligence a computational account as sparse conditional computation over distributed scientific contexts. This account distinguishes when a scaled standalone agent can match the network from when independent expertise and non-mergeable contexts make the network irreducible.

AI.tldr: Mycelium is a networked intelligence system that connects researchers and AI agents as multi-user co-scientists, automatically routing insights and hypotheses between humans and AI systems to accelerate scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: Current AI-for-science systems focus on scaling individual reasoning processes but ignore that challenging scientific problems are solved by teams with diverse expertise. The key challenge is cultivating networked intelligence - scaling connections between humans and AI systems so insights can reach the right people/agents who can act on them.
AI.method: Introduces Mycelium, an active shared workspace that automatically connects researchers and AI agents as multi-user co-scientists. The system captures observations and hypotheses as users/agents work, tracks how they relate to the team's evolving model, and routes them to the person/agent whose decisions they can inform.
AI.result: Evaluated in a biological multi-omics campaign where routed shared context turned a local analytical finding into a cross-expert mechanistic constraint and ultimately into an experimental design. Also provides a computational account of networked intelligence as sparse conditional computation over distributed scientific contexts.
AI.conclusion: Networked intelligence is essential for scientific discovery, with Mycelium demonstrating how to scale connections between humans and AI systems. The computational account helps distinguish when scaled standalone agents suffice versus when independent expertise and non-mergeable contexts make networked approaches irreducible.

title: CoDiffGRN: Rethinking Gene Regulatory Network Inference via the BEELINE-KGC Benchmark and Co-evolutionary Discrete Diffusion
summary: Inferring gene regulatory networks (GRNs) from single-cell transcriptomic data is crucial for biological discovery, yet existing approaches suffer from a fundamental misalignment with real-world needs. Researchers typically seek a small set of high-confidence regulatory interactions for experimental validation, often involving previously unseen genes. However, current benchmarks rely on transductive splits with global classification metrics, while prevailing models struggle to generalize under inductive settings. To bridge this gap, we reformulate GRN inference as an inductive, ranking-centric graph completion problem and introduce \textbf{\benchmark}, a new benchmark that incorporates an inductive gene-holdout split together with knowledge graph completion metrics to better evaluate top-ranked predictions. Building on this, we propose \textbf{\method}, the first co-evolutionary discrete diffusion framework that jointly models biologically coherent discretized gene expression states and regulatory interactions for robust inductive generalization and improved top-ranked regulatory discovery. We further introduce TF-ALL Subgraph Sampling (TASS) for scalable training. Extensive experiments on {\benchmark} show that {\method} establishes new state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in novel regulatory discovery, and ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: GRN-Bench: A new benchmark and method for gene regulatory network inference that reformulates the problem as inductive graph completion with ranking-focused evaluation, addressing limitations of existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Current GRN inference approaches are misaligned with real-world needs: researchers seek small sets of high-confidence interactions for experimental validation, often involving novel genes, but existing methods struggle with inductive generalization and use inappropriate benchmarks with transductive splits and global classification metrics.
AI.method: Proposes a co-evolutionary discrete diffusion framework that jointly models discretized gene expression states and regulatory interactions for robust inductive generalization, along with TF-ALL Subgraph Sampling (TASS) for scalable training.
AI.result: The method establishes new state-of-the-art performance on the proposed benchmark, significantly outperforming existing methods in novel regulatory discovery. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the design choices.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully bridges the gap between GRN inference methods and practical research needs by introducing a benchmark with inductive gene-holdout splits and a novel diffusion framework that enables robust generalization and improved top-ranked regulatory interaction discovery.

title: AI-Native Insurance for Agentic AI: Pricing, Underwriting, and End-to-End Automation
summary: Agentic AI introduces new insurance challenges because autonomous AI systems can make decisions, invoke tools, modify external environments, and interact with third-party services. This paper develops an AI-native mathematical framework for underwriting, pricing, and contract design for agentic AI deployments. A deployment is represented by a risk state that captures autonomy level, operational authority, permission exposure, governance maturity, and dependency concentration. The framework maps the risk state to event probabilities, loss severities, governance costs, premiums, deductibles, coverage allocation, and policy covenants, and formulates an optimization problem for insurance contract design under participation, profitability, and incentive compatibility constraints. The paper establishes structural properties of insurability, including characterization of an insurability region, monotone deterioration of feasibility with increasing exposure, and governance certification thresholds. Insurance is further interpreted as both an operational cost and a regulatory mechanism for AI deployment. A healthcare case study illustrates contract optimization, sensitivity analysis, and automated claims processing for agentic AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an AI-native mathematical framework for underwriting, pricing, and contract design specifically for agentic AI systems, addressing their unique risks through risk state modeling, contract optimization, and establishing insurability conditions.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems introduce novel insurance challenges due to their autonomous decision-making, ability to invoke tools, modify environments, and interact with third-party services, requiring specialized insurance frameworks beyond traditional approaches.
AI.method: Develops a mathematical framework representing AI deployments through risk states capturing autonomy level, operational authority, permission exposure, governance maturity, and dependency concentration. Maps risk states to probabilities, losses, costs, and insurance parameters, then formulates an optimization problem for contract design under participation, profitability, and incentive compatibility constraints.
AI.result: Establishes structural properties of insurability including characterization of insurability regions, monotone deterioration of feasibility with increasing exposure, and governance certification thresholds. Demonstrates the framework through a healthcare case study showing contract optimization, sensitivity analysis, and automated claims processing capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Insurance serves dual purposes for agentic AI systems: as an operational cost and as a regulatory mechanism for deployment. The proposed AI-native framework enables systematic underwriting, pricing, and contract design tailored to the unique risks of autonomous AI systems.

title: ShortOPD: Recovering Pruned LLMs with Short-to-Long On-Policy Distillation
summary: Structured pruning is a hardware-friendly way to compress LLMs, but it is mostly validated on multiple-choice recognition tasks, while the same compressed checkpoints can collapse on the free-form generation that deployment actually requires. Two observations trace this gap. First, greedy \textsc{pass}@$1$ nearly vanishes after compression, yet \textsc{pass}@$k$ recovers substantially under repeated sampling: useful generations are demoted, not erased. Second, the recoverable regime fails mainly through suffix repetition. Recovery should therefore train on the compressed model's own on-policy states with dense token-level supervision, which On-Policy Distillation (OPD) provides by reusing the pre-compression model as a frozen teacher. However, long on-policy rollouts spend early recovery budget on low-information repetitive suffixes, delaying loss descent. To mitigate this waste, we propose \textbf{\shortopd}, a short-to-long OPD schedule that detects teacher-confirmed repetitive suffixes, treats the surviving prefix as each rollout's effective length, and allocates future rollout budgets to the effective lengths the policy can currently use. Across math, code, and open-ended generation, \shortopd\ raises the compressed model's score to about $9\times$ its unrecovered value and $1.6$--$4.4\times$ standard recovery recipes (SFT w/o KD, KD, and SeqKD), and it matches a fixed $8192$-token rollout horizon within two points using a quarter of the training time ($8.5$ vs.\ $35.9$ hours) and $71\%$ fewer rollout tokens. We hope this recipe helps move structured pruning beyond marginal gains on perplexity and multiple-choice benchmarks, a step closer to deployment-ready generation quality.

AI.tldr: Short-to-long On-Policy Distillation (shortOPD) improves compressed LLMs' free-form generation by detecting repetitive suffixes, focusing training on effective prefixes, and matching long-rollout performance with 4x faster training.
AI.motivation: Structured pruning compresses LLMs but often fails on free-form generation tasks despite working on multiple-choice tasks. Two key observations: 1) useful generations are demoted but recoverable under repeated sampling, and 2) recoverable regimes fail mainly through suffix repetition.
