Standard recovery methods waste training budget on low-information repetitive suffixes.
AI.method: Proposes shortOPD (short-to-long On-Policy Distillation): detects teacher-confirmed repetitive suffixes, treats surviving prefix as effective rollout length, and allocates future rollout budgets to effective lengths the policy can currently use. Uses on-policy distillation with pre-compression model as frozen teacher.
AI.result: Across math, code, and open-ended generation, shortOPD raises compressed model's score to ~9× its unrecovered value and 1.6-4.4× standard recovery recipes (SFT w/o KD, KD, and SeqKD). Matches fixed 8192-token rollout performance within 2 points using 25% of training time (8.5 vs. 35.9 hours) and 71% fewer rollout tokens.
AI.conclusion: shortOPD helps move structured pruning beyond marginal gains on perplexity and multiple-choice benchmarks toward deployment-ready generation quality by efficiently recovering generation capability through focused training on meaningful prefixes.

title: Cost-Optimal Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio for Transportation Management
summary: Foundation models, including large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs), are increasingly used for transportation management center (TMC) tasks such as anomaly detection, incident reporting, and traveler information. Deploying multiple such models across TMC functions raises a portfolio question: which model should serve each function, in which deployment mode, and under what shared hardware budget? We formulate this as the Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio (FMDP) problem, a mixed-integer program minimizing total cost of ownership (TCO) subject to per-function quality, latency, and safety constraints over shared GPU capacity. We prove the problem NP-hard by reduction from the 0-1 knapsack problem and propose a polynomial-time greedy heuristic. In an illustrative case study with five TMC functions and 19 candidate (model, mode) pairs, FMDP identifies a mixed portfolio costing $34/mo (97% below the cheapest feasible all-closed-API baseline) by routing four functions to open-source APIs and the one function whose quality floor no open-source model meets to a closed API. Break-even analysis shows that on-premise GPU investment becomes reasonable only above approximately 309 vision queries/hour or if API prices double.

AI.tldr: A framework for optimally deploying multiple foundation models across transportation management functions to minimize costs while meeting quality and latency constraints.
AI.motivation: As foundation models (LLMs/VLMs) become widely used in transportation management centers for tasks like anomaly detection and incident reporting, there's a need to determine which models to deploy for each function, what deployment modes to use, and how to allocate shared hardware resources efficiently to minimize costs.
AI.method: Formulated the Foundation Model Deployment Portfolio (FMDP) as a mixed-integer programming problem minimizing total cost of ownership subject to quality, latency, and safety constraints over shared GPU capacity. Proved NP-hardness via reduction to 0-1 knapsack and developed a polynomial-time greedy heuristic solution.
AI.result: In a case study with 5 TMC functions and 19 candidate model-mode pairs, FMDP identified a mixed portfolio costing $34/month (97% below cheapest all-closed-API baseline), routing 4 functions to open-source APIs and 1 critical function to closed API. Break-even analysis showed on-premise GPU investment becomes reasonable above ~309 vision queries/hour or if API prices double.
AI.conclusion: The FMDP framework enables efficient deployment of foundation models in transportation management by balancing open-source and closed APIs, significantly reducing costs while meeting operational requirements, with clear thresholds for when on-premise GPU investment becomes cost-effective.

title: HEDGEHOG: Hierarchical Evaluation of Drug Generators Through Rigorous Filtration
comment: 29 pages (including References and Appendix sections), 8 tables, 7 figures, 2 supplementary files
summary: Generative molecular models can support early drug discovery by proposing new candidate compounds de novo. In practice, useful candidates must balance target-relevant activity, synthetic accessibility, physicochemical properties, and other multiparameter design constraints. However, metrics commonly used to evaluate molecular generators only weakly reflect whether the generated compounds are medicinally plausible and suitable for downstream computation. This can produce false positives in model evaluation, incorrect assumptions, and inefficient use of computational resources. We introduce HEDGEHOG, a unified six-stage filtration benchmark that is inspired by industrial hit identification workflows: (i) preprocessing; (ii) physicochemical descriptor screening; (iii) structural alerts and graph-sanity checks; (iv) synthesis feasibility; (v) docking and binding affinity estimation; and (vi) three-dimensional pose and interaction checks. We evaluate 23 molecular generators across three model classes under a standardized protocol. Across 230,000 generated molecules, only 0.65% of initial molecules survive all stages. Our results expose a central limitation of current molecular generators: molecules that appear acceptable under isolated criteria rarely satisfy medicinal chemistry, synthesis, docking, and 3D pose filters simultaneously.

AI.tldr: HEDGEHOG is a six-stage filtration benchmark inspired by industrial drug discovery workflows that reveals only 0.65% of molecules generated by current molecular models survive all practical medicinal chemistry, synthesis, docking, and 3D pose requirements.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation metrics for generative molecular models weakly reflect whether generated compounds are medicinally plausible and suitable for downstream computation, leading to false positives in model evaluation, incorrect assumptions, and inefficient use of computational resources.
AI.method: Developed HEDGEHOG, a unified six-stage filtration benchmark inspired by industrial hit identification workflows: (1) preprocessing, (2) physicochemical descriptor screening, (3) structural alerts and graph-sanity checks, (4) synthesis feasibility, (5) docking and binding affinity estimation, and (6) three-dimensional pose and interaction checks. Evaluated 23 molecular generators across three model classes under standardized protocol across 230,000 generated molecules.
AI.result: Only 0.65% of initial generated molecules survived all six filtration stages, revealing that molecules appearing acceptable under isolated criteria rarely satisfy medicinal chemistry, synthesis, docking, and 3D pose filters simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: The study exposes a central limitation of current molecular generators and demonstrates the need for more realistic evaluation frameworks that incorporate practical medicinal chemistry and drug discovery constraints to avoid false positives and computational inefficiencies.

title: Harness Handbook: Making Evolving Agent Harnesses Readable,Navigable, and Editable
summary: The capability of a modern AI agent depends not only on its foundation model but also on its harness, which constructs prompts, manages state, invokes tools, and coordinates execution. As models, APIs, environments, and requirements evolve, the harness must be continually modified. Before such a change can be made, a developer or coding agent must identify all code locations that implement the target behavior. This is difficult because production harnesses are large, tightly coupled, and behaviorally distributed, while modification requests describe what the system should do and repositories are organized by files and modules. Code search, repository indexing, and long-context processing ease inspection, but still leave this behavior-to-code mapping to be recovered by hand. Behavior localization is therefore a central bottleneck in harness evolution. We introduce the Harness Handbook, a behavior-centric representation synthesized automatically from a harness codebase via static analysis and LLM-assisted structuring, linking each behavior to its corresponding source. We also introduce Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD), which guides agents from high-level behaviors to relevant implementation details and verifies candidate locations against the current source. On diverse modification requests from two open-source harnesses, Handbook-Assisted planning improves behavior localization and edit-plan quality while using fewer planner tokens, with the largest gains on scattered sites, rarely executed paths, and cross-module interactions. Evolving complex agentic systems thus depends not only on generating edits, but also on determining where those edits should be made.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Harness Handbook, a behavior-centric representation that automatically maps behaviors to code locations in AI agent harnesses, and Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure to help developers/agents locate relevant implementation details for making modifications.
AI.motivation: AI agent harnesses (which handle prompts, state, tools, and execution) need constant modification as models, APIs, environments, and requirements evolve. However, identifying all code locations implementing a specific behavior is difficult because harnesses are large, tightly coupled, and behaviorally distributed. Current approaches (code search, repository indexing, long-context processing) still require manual behavior-to-code mapping, making behavior localization a bottleneck in harness evolution.
AI.method: Two main contributions: 1) Harness Handbook - a behavior-centric representation automatically synthesized from harness code via static analysis and LLM-assisted structuring that links behaviors to source code. 2) Behavior-Guided Progressive Disclosure (BGPD) - guides agents from high-level behaviors to relevant implementation details and verifies candidate locations against current source.
AI.result: On diverse modification requests from two open-source harnesses, Handbook-Assisted planning improves behavior localization and edit-plan quality while using fewer planner tokens. The largest gains are observed on scattered sites, rarely executed paths, and cross-module interactions.
AI.conclusion: Evolving complex agentic systems requires not only generating edits but also determining where those edits should be made. The Harness Handbook with BGPD addresses the behavior localization bottleneck, enabling more efficient and accurate modifications to AI agent harnesses.

title: SteinGate: Tail-Sensitive Safe Reinforcement Learning via Stein Discrepancy
comment: Accepted for Publication at the 42nd Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI), 2026
summary: Safe reinforcement learning typically enforces safety by bounding expected cumulative costs, a criterion that often fails to detect rare but catastrophic tail events. To overcome these limitations, this paper introduces SteinGate, a boundary-aware distributional safety certificate that replaces fragile tail fitting with a robust consistency check using Kernelized Stein Discrepancy while accounting for boundary atoms induced by clipped costs. SteinGate evaluates whether observed policy rollout costs remain consistent with a safe reference distribution, providing a non-parametric safety certificate. This certificate is used to dynamically adapt the learning regime: favoring reward-improving policy updates when rollouts remain consistent with the safe reference and switching to recovery behavior when the cost tail deviates. Experiments on continuous-control benchmarks demonstrate that SteinGate significantly reduces both the frequency and severity of constraint violations during training while maintaining competitive returns relative to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: SteinGate introduces a distributional safety certificate using Kernelized Stein Discrepancy to detect rare catastrophic events in RL, replacing fragile tail fitting with robust consistency checks, enabling dynamic regime switching between reward optimization and safety recovery.
AI.motivation: Current safe RL methods bound expected cumulative costs but fail to detect rare but catastrophic tail events. There's a need for safety certificates that can identify these dangerous tail distributions without relying on fragile parametric tail fitting.
AI.method: SteinGate uses Kernelized Stein Discrepancy to check consistency between observed policy rollout costs and a safe reference distribution, accounting for boundary atoms from clipped costs. This creates a non-parametric safety certificate that dynamically adapts learning: favoring reward improvements when safe, switching to recovery behavior when cost tails deviate.
AI.result: Experiments on continuous-control benchmarks show SteinGate significantly reduces both frequency and severity of constraint violations during training while maintaining competitive returns compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: SteinGate provides an effective distributional safety approach that robustly handles rare catastrophic events through non-parametric consistency checking, enabling safer RL training without sacrificing performance.

title: Theory-Level Autoformalization: From Isolated Statements to Unified Formal Knowledge Bases
summary: Autoformalization translates informal natural language into formal, machine-verifiable languages. While most work focuses on individual statements, real formalization efforts are inherently theory-level: they require an entire web of axioms, definitions, and lemmas before target theorems can even be stated. In this position paper, we argue for theory-level autoformalization: formalizing complete theories, including all their inter-dependencies, as structured libraries. We examine the significance of this shift, address alternative views, identify open challenges, and propose three promising paths forward. Our survey of autoformalization is available at https://github.com/marcusm117/Awesome-Autoformalization.

AI.tldr: Autoformalization needs to shift from statement-level to theory-level approaches, formalizing complete theories with all their dependencies as structured libraries.
AI.motivation: Current autoformalization work focuses on individual statements, but real formalization requires entire webs of axioms, definitions, and lemmas before target theorems can be stated, making theory-level approaches necessary for practical applications.
AI.method: This is a position paper that examines the significance of shifting to theory-level autoformalization, addresses alternative views, identifies open challenges, and proposes three promising paths forward for formalizing complete theories as structured libraries.
AI.result: The paper argues for theory-level autoformalization as a necessary evolution beyond statement-level approaches, providing a framework for future research and practical implementation of formalizing entire theories with all their inter-dependencies.
AI.conclusion: Autoformalization must transition to theory-level approaches to be practically useful, requiring formalization of complete theories as structured libraries, with significant implications for future research directions and applications in automated theorem proving and formal verification.

title: Concurrent Image Understanding and Generation: Self-Correcting Coupled Markov Jump Processes
summary: Human cognition does not separate understanding and generation. A teacher at a whiteboard speaks and draws $\textit{together}$, each modality reshapes the other. In this paper, we bring this coupled loop to artificial systems. Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) are ideally suited to this task, yet existing samplers either decode text and image interleavedly or independently update them in parallel branches that share only previous-step history, but not the other modality's latest decisions $\textit{within}$ the same step; combined with MDMs' inability to remask, cross-modal contradictions are neither detected nor repaired. We introduce $\textbf{Self-Correcting Coupled Markov Jump Processes (SC-CMJP)}$, a framework in which one modality's transition rates are functionals of the other modality's confidence score, as weighted by cross-modal attention. Furthermore, a remasking jump retracts commitments the moment cross-modal evidence turns against them. In conjunction with SC-CMJP, we introduce $\texttt{CO}_\texttt{2}\texttt{Jump}$ (Self-$\underline{\text{CO}}$rrecting $\underline{\text{CO}}$upled $\underline{\text{Jump}}$), a novel training-free single-pass sampler for joint multimodal geneneration. For training and evaluation purposes, we have created and will release three large-scale joint multimodal generation corpora: $\text{JEdit-1M}$, $\text{JMaze-200K}$, $\text{JNono-200K}$, with matching in- and out-of-distribution benchmarks. $\texttt{CO}_\texttt{2}\texttt{Jump}$ achieves best joint performance for image understanding and editing as well as visual reasoning (maze and nonogram solving). The performance of the sampler scales monotonically with the number of denoising steps, evidence that the benefits of cross-modal coupling $\textit{compound}$ across the trajectory. Project page: https://coupled-jump.github.io

AI.tldr: A training-free single-pass sampler for joint multimodal generation that enables text and image modalities to mutually correct each other during generation using cross-modal attention and remasking capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal generation systems process text and image modalities either interleavedly or in parallel branches that only share previous-step history, not current-step decisions, leading to cross-modal contradictions that cannot be detected or repaired due to masked diffusion models' inability to remask.
AI.method: Introduces Self-Correcting Coupled Markov Jump Processes (SC-CMJP), where one modality's transition rates depend on the other modality's confidence scores weighted by cross-modal attention. Also introduces CO₂Jump, a training-free single-pass sampler with remasking jumps that retract commitments when cross-modal evidence contradicts them.
AI.result: CO₂Jump achieves best joint performance for image understanding/editing and visual reasoning (maze and nonogram solving) across three large-scale joint multimodal generation corpora (JEdit-1M, JMaze-200K, JNono-200K). Performance scales monotonically with denoising steps, showing benefits of cross-modal coupling compound across trajectory.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SC-CMJP framework and CO₂Jump sampler successfully bring human-like coupled generation to AI systems, enabling text and image modalities to reshape each other in real-time during generation, with measurable performance improvements that scale with computation.

title: EZSMT Version 3, Matured
comment: Under consideration in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)
summary: Constraint Answer Set Programming (CASP) is a hybrid reasoning paradigm that combines Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Constraint Processing and Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT), enabling powerful declarative encodings of complex combinatorial search problems. This paper presents the design and implementation of EZSMTV3, an extensible SMT-based CASP framework that advances the translational approach to CASP solving. Building upon the foundation of the EZSMT+ system, EZSMTV3 introduces a more expressive input language, supports optimization via weak constraints, and offers foundations for streamlined integration of new constraint types. Rather than implementing custom search procedures, EZSMTV3 leverages state-of-the-art SMT solvers, such as CVC5, YICES, and Z3 to perform reasoning. The paper provides benchmarking results comparing EZSMTV3 with its CASP peers such as CLINGCON, CLINGO[DL], and CLINGO[LP], while showcasing its ability to handle mixed-domain constraints involving both integers and reals. The system provides a robust platform for future extensions and theoretical exploration within the CASP domain.

AI.tldr: EZSMTV3 is an extensible SMT-based Constraint Answer Set Programming framework that advances the translational approach by leveraging state-of-the-art SMT solvers for reasoning, supporting mixed-domain constraints and optimization.
AI.motivation: Constraint Answer Set Programming (CASP) combines ASP with constraint processing and SMT for declarative encodings of complex combinatorial problems, but existing systems may lack expressiveness, optimization support, and streamlined integration of new constraint types.
AI.method: The authors designed EZSMTV3 as an extensible SMT-based CASP framework that uses a translational approach rather than custom search procedures. It leverages state-of-the-art SMT solvers (CVC5, YICES, Z3) for reasoning, introduces a more expressive input language, supports optimization via weak constraints, and provides foundations for easy integration of new constraint types.
AI.result: EZSMTV3 successfully handles mixed-domain constraints involving both integers and reals, and benchmarking shows it performs competitively with existing CASP systems like CLINGCON, CLINGO[DL], and CLINGO[LP]. The system provides a robust platform for future extensions.
AI.conclusion: EZSMTV3 represents a significant advancement in CASP solving by providing an extensible, SMT-based framework that leverages existing SMT solver technology while offering enhanced expressiveness, optimization support, and a foundation for future theoretical exploration and extensions in the CASP domain.

title: EMAGN: Efficient Multi-Attention Graph Network via Learned Clustering for Scalable Traffic Forecasting
summary: Traffic forecasting is highly challenging due to complex and nonlinear spatial and temporal dependencies. Self-attention mechanisms have been widely adopted to model dynamic and long-range dependencies, achieving state-of-the-art performance, but suffer from limited scalability due to quadratic computational and memory complexity. To address this, we propose an Efficient Multi-Attention Graph Network (EMAGN) that linearises the spatial attention mechanism itself, inspired by the theory of fast high-dimensional Gaussian filtering. Two learned clustering matrices C_k and C_v adaptively group key and value vectors into M super-clusters, reducing complexity from O(N^2 d) to O(NMd) without sacrificing the flexibility of attention for dynamic dependency modelling. Experimental results on PEMS-BAY and METR-LA show that EMAGN achieves accuracy within 2.7-3.2% MAE of full-attention GMAN while reducing training time by 32%, inference time by 38%, and GPU memory by 58%. Critically, at K=16 attention heads, full-attention GMAN runs out of memory on a standard 11 GB GPU entirely while EMAGN continues to operate, demonstrating a categorical expansion of feasible model configurations. EMAGN also surpasses Linformer and Performer in both accuracy and efficiency within the same backbone, owing to its traffic-network-aware adaptive clustering.

AI.tldr: EMAGN proposes an efficient multi-attention graph network that linearizes spatial attention through adaptive clustering, achieving near full-attention accuracy with dramatically reduced computational cost and memory usage for traffic forecasting.
AI.motivation: Self-attention mechanisms for traffic forecasting suffer from quadratic computational and memory complexity, limiting scalability. While effective for modeling dynamic spatial-temporal dependencies, they become impractical for larger networks or more attention heads due to resource constraints.
AI.method: EMAGN linearizes spatial attention using learned clustering matrices C_k and C_v that adaptively group key and value vectors into M super-clusters. This reduces complexity from O(N²d) to O(NMd) while maintaining attention's flexibility. Inspired by fast high-dimensional Gaussian filtering theory, the approach enables efficient dynamic dependency modeling without sacrificing performance.
AI.result: On PEMS-BAY and METR-LA datasets, EMAGN achieves accuracy within 2.7-3.2% MAE of full-attention GMAN while reducing training time by 32%, inference time by 38%, and GPU memory by 58%. Critically, at K=16 attention heads, full-attention GMAN runs out of memory on an 11GB GPU while EMAGN continues to operate. EMAGN also outperforms Linformer and Performer in accuracy and efficiency within the same backbone.
AI.conclusion: EMAGN successfully addresses the scalability limitations of self-attention for traffic forecasting through adaptive clustering, enabling much larger and more complex models to be deployed within practical computational constraints while maintaining competitive accuracy and traffic-network-aware performance.

title: Set-shifting Behavioral Test for Harnessed Agents
summary: What happens to an LLM agent's tool choice when the reliable tool silently changes within an ongoing session? We borrow set-shifting from cognitive psychology to study how well agents adapt to hidden reliability shifts. Our benchmark mounts tool-skill libraries with redundancies, where many tools solve the same task but differ in hidden reliability. In our evaluation framework, a branched schedule shifts the reliable tool group at hidden boundaries and pairs every shift with a no-shift control. We find that agents, by default, settle on a small recurring routine within a few turns of each boundary, with call shares concentrating on a few discrete values after each reliability shift. We score the set-shifting accuracy for each agent trajectory: the joint probability of routing to the target tool group in every post-shift window. We test open-weight LLMs in an open-source agentic harness and find qualitatively distinct failure modes across the same set of routines. We also find that set framing, how the toolset presents the alternatives as competing or complementary, shifts the routing dynamics.

AI.tldr: LLM agents tend to settle into small recurring routines and struggle to adapt when reliable tools change silently during sessions, exhibiting discrete failure modes in set-shifting scenarios.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLM agents handle hidden reliability shifts in tools during ongoing sessions, borrowing the concept of set-shifting from cognitive psychology to study adaptation to changing tool reliability.
AI.method: Created a benchmark with tool-skill libraries containing redundant tools that solve the same tasks but differ in hidden reliability. Used a branched schedule that shifts the reliable tool group at hidden boundaries, paired with no-shift controls. Evaluated set-shifting accuracy by measuring joint probability of routing to target tool group in post-shift windows.
AI.result: Agents settle into small recurring routines within a few turns of boundaries, with tool usage concentrating on discrete values after reliability shifts. Open-weight LLMs in open-source agentic harness show qualitatively distinct failure modes across same routines. Set framing (presenting alternatives as competing or complementary) affects routing dynamics.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents exhibit systematic patterns in how they handle hidden tool reliability changes, with both behavioral regularities and distinct failure modes, suggesting current agent architectures have limitations in adapting to dynamic tool availability changes.

title: Reassessing Muon for Matrix Factorization
summary: Muon has recently emerged as a strong optimizer for large-scale deep learning, where it reshapes gradient updates through approximate orthogonalization and has been reported to outperform Adam and AdamW in large language model training. Its empirical success has motivated a growing body of theoretical work that interprets Muon as steepest descent under the spectral norm. Yet it remains unclear which of Muon's advantages stem from its update rule itself and which are artifacts of the scale, architecture, and data of modern deep networks. In this work, we isolate the optimizer from these confounding factors by studying Muon on a simple, well-understood, and spectrally structured problem: low-rank matrix factorization. Through a controlled comparison against carefully tuned adaptive baselines, we find that Muon does not consistently outperform AdamW in this setting and that several previously reported advantages are sensitive to hyperparameter choices. Our results provide a more nuanced picture of when spectrum-aware orthogonalization is beneficial and argue for evaluating modern optimizers on controlled problems in addition to end-to-end benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Muon optimizer's advantages over AdamW are inconsistent and hyperparameter-sensitive in controlled low-rank matrix factorization experiments, challenging claims of universal superiority.
AI.motivation: While Muon has shown empirical success in large-scale deep learning (outperforming Adam/AdamW for LLMs), it's unclear whether its advantages come from the optimizer itself or confounding factors like scale, architecture, and data. The authors want to isolate Muon's effects in a controlled setting.
AI.method: Studied Muon on a simple, well-understood spectrally structured problem: low-rank matrix factorization. Conducted controlled comparisons against carefully tuned adaptive baselines (AdamW) to isolate optimizer effects.
AI.result: Muon does not consistently outperform AdamW in low-rank matrix factorization. Several previously reported advantages of Muon are sensitive to hyperparameter choices.
AI.conclusion: The benefits of spectrum-aware orthogonalization (Muon's approach) are more nuanced than previously thought, and modern optimizers should be evaluated on controlled problems in addition to end-to-end benchmarks to better understand their true advantages.

title: LAPO: Leave-One-Turn Attribution for Self-Generated Process Rewards in Multi-Turn Search Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning for multi-turn search reasoning typically relies on terminal outcome rewards, which cannot distinguish useful, redundant, and harmful intermediate interactions. We propose LAPO, a self-generated process-supervision method based on backward leave-one-turn attribution. For each search turn, LAPO replaces the turn and its retrieval observation with a fixed [DELETE] placeholder and measures the resulting change in the current policy's mean log-likelihood of the gold answer. This Answer-Likelihood Gain estimates the turn's contribution while preserving all downstream interactions, allowing early evidence to be evaluated in the complete reasoning context. LAPO further applies sign-consistency gating, retaining only normalized process advantages whose directions agree with their raw attribution scores. The method requires no additional reward model, teacher, verifier, or LLM-as-a-Judge. Across seven knowledge-intensive question-answering datasets with local retrieval, LAPO achieves an average exact-match score of 0.326, outperforming the strongest step-reward baseline, IGPO, by 0.053. Ablations show complementary benefits from backward attribution and sign-consistency gating, demonstrating that policy-derived retrospective attribution can provide effective process supervision for multi-turn search agents.

AI.tldr: LAPO is a self-generated process-supervision method for multi-turn search agents that uses backward leave-one-turn attribution to estimate each search turn's contribution to the final answer without needing external reward models or judges.
AI.motivation: Traditional reinforcement learning for multi-turn search relies on terminal outcome rewards, which fail to distinguish between useful, redundant, and harmful intermediate interactions. This makes it difficult to properly evaluate and learn from intermediate reasoning steps.
AI.method: LAPO uses backward leave-one-turn attribution: for each search turn, it replaces the turn and its retrieval observation with a [DELETE] placeholder and measures the change in the policy's mean log-likelihood of the gold answer. This Answer-Likelihood Gain estimates each turn's contribution while preserving downstream interactions. It also applies sign-consistency gating to retain only normalized process advantages that agree with their raw attribution scores.
AI.result: Across seven knowledge-intensive question-answering datasets with local retrieval, LAPO achieves an average exact-match score of 0.326, outperforming the strongest step-reward baseline IGPO by 0.053. Ablations show complementary benefits from both backward attribution and sign-consistency gating.
AI.conclusion: Policy-derived retrospective attribution can provide effective process supervision for multi-turn search agents. LAPO successfully addresses the limitation of terminal rewards by enabling turn-level evaluation without requiring additional reward models, teachers, verifiers, or LLM judges.

title: Deconstructing Actor-Critic: A Large-scale Empirical Study of Design Components for Practitioners
summary: Reinforcement learning is increasingly being considered for controlling real-world systems, from fusion plasma and autonomous vehicles to drug discovery and drinking water treatment, where reliability is essential and tuning budgets are limited. Actor-critic algorithms share a set of design decisions, such as how the policy is updated, how it represents the distribution over actions, how its gradient is estimated, and how often it is updated relative to the value estimator. Using a control task derived from a real water treatment plant, we analyze over 33,000 experiments to determine how these components affect variability across runs and sensitivity to hyperparameters. Common defaults, such as Gaussian action distributions with pathwise gradient estimators, are among the least reliable configurations, whereas bounded distributions with adaptive update schedules remain robust across a wide range of settings. These findings offer empirical guidance to practitioners across scientific and engineering domains for understanding and making component-level decisions when adapting actor-critic methods to new real-world control settings.

AI.tldr: Actor-critic RL algorithms for real-world control tasks show that common defaults (Gaussian distributions, pathwise gradients) are unreliable, while bounded distributions with adaptive update schedules are more robust across 33,000+ experiments on a water treatment control task.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning is being applied to real-world systems where reliability is essential and tuning budgets are limited, but actor-critic algorithms have many design choices that affect performance variability and sensitivity to hyperparameters.
AI.method: Analyzed over 33,000 experiments on a control task derived from a real water treatment plant to evaluate how different actor-critic components affect variability across runs and sensitivity to hyperparameters, comparing various policy update methods, action distributions, gradient estimators, and update schedules.
AI.result: Common defaults (Gaussian action distributions with pathwise gradient estimators) are among the least reliable configurations, while bounded distributions with adaptive update schedules remain robust across a wide range of settings.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide empirical guidance for practitioners in scientific and engineering domains to understand and make component-level decisions when adapting actor-critic methods to new real-world control settings, suggesting bounded distributions with adaptive updates over traditional defaults.

title: How Far Can Root Cause Analysis Go on Real-World Telemetry Data?
summary: Identifying root causes in production microservice failures requires reasoning over large-scale, multimodal telemetry spanning metrics, logs, and traces, a problem that has proved resistant to both classical and LLM-based approaches. The OpenRCA dataset exemplifies these challenges: it is large-scale, multimodal, and lacks detailed domain knowledge, and yields consistently low accuracy across all existing methods. We show that classical causal discovery methods and existing LLM-based multi-agent systems fail to reliably identify root causes on this benchmark, and present a Structured Multi-Agent RCA pipeline that substantially outperforms existing LLM-based and classical baselines, supporting both domain-knowledge and knowledge-free operating modes. To diagnose where failures originate, we introduce a reverse reasoning agent that, given the correct answer, identifies which signals in the extracted anomalies support it and determines whether Stage~1 had access to those signals, classifying each failure as Reasoning Gap (evidence present but unused) or Data Ambiguity (evidence genuinely absent). This analysis reveals that the required evidence is present in the vast majority of failures: the bottleneck is not data access but the agent's ability to reason over it correctly. We further introduce an automated rule mining pipeline that systematically extracts discrimination rules from reverse reasoning reports, reducing reliance on manual knowledge curation. Across all configurations, model reasoning capability and domain knowledge are the primary constraints: stronger models embed more domain expertise, and explicit knowledge injection partially compensates for this gap. Reasoning performance remains practically bounded even when evidence extraction is perfect: scaffold engineering and better data pipelines alone cannot close this gap; progress requires improvements at the model level.

AI.tldr: A Structured Multi-Agent RCA pipeline that uses reverse reasoning and automated rule mining substantially outperforms existing methods for identifying root causes in production microservice failures, showing that reasoning capability rather than data access is the primary bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Production microservice failure root cause analysis is challenging due to large-scale, multimodal telemetry (metrics, logs, traces) and lack of domain knowledge. Existing classical and LLM-based methods perform poorly on benchmarks like OpenRCA dataset, which exemplifies these challenges with consistently low accuracy across all current approaches.
AI.method: Proposed Structured Multi-Agent RCA pipeline with reverse reasoning agent that, given correct answers, identifies which anomaly signals support them and determines if evidence was accessible. Also introduces automated rule mining pipeline that extracts discrimination rules from reverse reasoning reports. The system supports both domain-knowledge and knowledge-free operating modes.
AI.result: The pipeline substantially outperforms existing LLM-based and classical baselines. Analysis shows evidence is present in vast majority of failures, with bottleneck being reasoning ability rather than data access. Model reasoning capability and domain knowledge are primary constraints - stronger models embed more domain expertise, and explicit knowledge injection partially compensates for reasoning gaps.
AI.conclusion: Progress in root cause analysis requires improvements at model level, as scaffold engineering and better data pipelines alone cannot overcome reasoning limitations. Reasoning performance remains practically bounded even with perfect evidence extraction, emphasizing the need for better reasoning capabilities in models rather than just improved data access or processing pipelines.

title: Tabular Foundation Models for Discrete Choice Estimation
summary: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) generate predictions on structured data via in-context learning, without task-specific estimation. We ask whether TFMs can be effectively applied to discrete choice, a central demand estimation framework in marketing and operations, and find that directly applying TFMs yields limited performance. The gap is structural: TFMs assume row-independent observations, whereas discrete choice is inherently set-valued and subject to persistent consumer preference heterogeneity. We propose a reformulation that encodes both choice-set dependence and individual heterogeneity within a row-based learning framework. Evaluated on a yogurt scanner panel, individual-level heterogeneity encoding is the dominant driver of predictive accuracy. The best reformulation outperforms hierarchical Bayesian estimation by 8\% in holdout log-likelihood and 3.6\% in hit rate, running 16 times faster, a practical advantage for large-scale demand estimation. The advantage is largest in the medium-data regime (10--40 purchase occasions per consumer), where parametric Bayesian shrinkage most distorts estimates for atypical consumers. Fine-tuning on population choice data provides additional gains for consumers with shallow purchase histories, where in-context learning has limited individual-specific signal to condition on. These results establish a principled approach for applying foundation models to consumer choice problems more broadly.

AI.tldr: TFMs can be effectively applied to discrete choice problems by reformulating them to encode choice-set dependence and individual heterogeneity, outperforming hierarchical Bayesian methods in both accuracy and speed.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models (TFMs) have limitations when directly applied to discrete choice problems because they assume row-independent observations, while discrete choice is inherently set-valued and subject to persistent consumer preference heterogeneity. The authors want to determine if TFMs can be effectively adapted for this important marketing and operations framework.
AI.method: The authors propose a reformulation that encodes both choice-set dependence and individual heterogeneity within a row-based learning framework. They evaluate their approach on yogurt scanner panel data, comparing it against hierarchical Bayesian estimation. They also explore fine-tuning on population choice data to improve performance for consumers with shallow purchase histories.
AI.result: The reformulated TFM approach outperforms hierarchical Bayesian estimation by 8% in holdout log-likelihood and 3.6% in hit rate, while running 16 times faster. Individual-level heterogeneity encoding was found to be the dominant driver of predictive accuracy. The advantage is largest in the medium-data regime (10-40 purchase occasions per consumer) where parametric Bayesian shrinkage most distorts estimates for atypical consumers.
AI.conclusion: TFMs can be successfully applied to consumer choice problems through appropriate reformulations that account for structural characteristics of discrete choice data, establishing a principled approach for using foundation models in marketing and operations demand estimation tasks.

title: Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning with Tool-Augmented Evidence for Urban Region Profiling
summary: Urban region profiling constitutes a core problem in urban computing, supporting applications such as population estimation, economic assessment, and environmental monitoring. Existing methods typically formulate this task as multimodal representation learning, fusing heterogeneous urban data, e.g., satellite imagery, points of interest, textual descriptions, and 3D building information, into latent embeddings for prediction. However, these approaches are largely correlation-driven, assume cross-modal consistency, and rely on static pipelines, which limit their robustness in heterogeneous or unseen urban regions. We propose UrbanAgent, an agentic framework that reframes urban region profiling as a reasoning-driven inference problem. UrbanAgent instantiates an independent agent for each data modality and performs structured multi-agent collaborative reasoning to explicitly address cross-modal inconsistencies rather than absorbing them into a single representation. In addition, UrbanAgent extends indicator prediction as a closed-loop process of active evidence acquisition and iterative reasoning, enabling agents to verify uncertain inferences through tool-augmented retrieval of external knowledge optimized via reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments on global urban datasets for Carbon emissions, GDP, and Population estimation show that UrbanAgent consistently outperforms existing baselines, achieving an average improvement of 8.1% in R2, and exhibiting strong generalization performance in unseen-city settings.

AI.tldr: UrbanAgent: An agentic framework that reframes urban region profiling as a reasoning-driven inference problem using multi-agent collaborative reasoning and active evidence acquisition, outperforming traditional correlation-driven multimodal methods.
AI.motivation: Existing urban region profiling methods are correlation-driven, assume cross-modal consistency, and rely on static pipelines, limiting robustness in heterogeneous or unseen urban regions. They fuse heterogeneous data into latent embeddings but don't address cross-modal inconsistencies effectively.
AI.method: UrbanAgent reframes urban region profiling as a reasoning-driven inference problem with an independent agent for each data modality (satellite imagery, POIs, textual descriptions, 3D building info). It performs structured multi-agent collaborative reasoning to explicitly address cross-modal inconsistencies rather than absorbing them into a single representation. The framework extends prediction as a closed-loop process of active evidence acquisition and iterative reasoning, enabling agents to verify uncertain inferences through tool-augmented retrieval of external knowledge optimized via reinforcement learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on global urban datasets for Carbon emissions, GDP, and Population estimation show that UrbanAgent consistently outperforms existing baselines, achieving an average improvement of 8.1% in R2 score. It exhibits strong generalization performance in unseen-city settings.
AI.conclusion: UrbanAgent represents a paradigm shift from correlation-driven multimodal representation learning to reasoning-driven inference for urban region profiling. The agentic framework with multi-modal collaborative reasoning and active evidence acquisition addresses limitations of existing methods and demonstrates superior performance and generalization capabilities.

title: Accuracy-Preserving Stability Regularization for Large-Scale Retail Demand Forecasting
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, accepted for presentation at ICEME 2026
summary: Retail demand forecasts are reused across replenishment, capacity, labor, and transportation planning cycles. Point-error objectives do not constrain abrupt movement between adjacent forecasts, while post-hoc smoothing acts only after model fitting. We ask whether a training-time penalty on consecutive within-series movement can improve horizontal forecast-path stability without materially changing point accuracy. The penalty is evaluated in a temporal-structured pipeline combining recent-demand embeddings with calendar, price, hierarchy, item, and store features. On selected M5 demand series at 1000, 3000, and 4000-series scales, the stability-aware hybrid model improves Forecast Stability Score over XGBoost by 6.91%, 6.66%, and 7.68%, respectively, while RMSE changes remain within 0.72% across three random seeds. Post-hoc exponential smoothing attains lower raw movement but incurs a larger RMSE cost; training-time regularization preserves more point accuracy and performs favorably under normalized stability. These findings extend forecast evaluation from point-error minimization toward an accuracy-stability trade-off perspective for operational retail forecasting.

AI.tldr: A training-time penalty on consecutive forecast movements improves horizontal forecast-path stability without significantly affecting point accuracy in retail demand forecasting.
AI.motivation: Retail demand forecasts are reused across multiple planning cycles (replenishment, capacity, labor, transportation), but point-error objectives don't constrain abrupt movements between adjacent forecasts, while post-hoc smoothing only acts after model fitting.
AI.method: Proposes a training-time penalty on consecutive within-series forecast movements, evaluated in a temporal-structured pipeline combining recent-demand embeddings with calendar, price, hierarchy, item, and store features. Tested on M5 demand series at different scales.
AI.result: The stability-aware hybrid model improves Forecast Stability Score over XGBoost by 6.91%, 6.66%, and 7.68% at different scales, while RMSE changes remain within 0.72%. Post-hoc exponential smoothing achieves lower raw movement but incurs larger RMSE cost.
AI.conclusion: Training-time regularization preserves more point accuracy and performs better under normalized stability metrics, extending forecast evaluation from point-error minimization toward an accuracy-stability trade-off perspective for operational retail forecasting.

title: AI advice suppresses people's willingness to say "I don't know", even when the advice is wrong and accuracy is incentivized
summary: Knowing when to say "I don't know" is fundamental to human judgment, yet AI assistants offer a fluent answer to almost any question. In five experiments (N = 3,132; four preregistered, one direct replication), participants answered difficult questions and could always decline to respond. We engineered the questions so that AI advice was wrong, separating AI use from its accuracy. Merely having access to AI nearly eliminated participants' willingness to suspend judgment, and this held whether the advice was actively requested or simply displayed. Consequently, participants answered more questions but were correct about a third as often as when AI was unavailable-yet their confidence nearly doubled. Incentivizing accuracy and penalizing inaccuracy led participants to seek and follow AI advice less, answer more accurately, and suspend judgment more often, though still far less than when AI was unavailable. As AI suggestions grow ubiquitous and unsolicited, they may not simply affect answer accuracy; they may even alter the metacognitive threshold at which people decide whether they know enough to answer.

AI.tldr: AI access reduces people's willingness to say "I don't know" even when AI advice is wrong, leading to more answers but dramatically lower accuracy with inflated confidence.
AI.motivation: AI assistants typically provide fluent answers to almost any question, but this may undermine human metacognitive processes for knowing when to suspend judgment. The researchers wanted to understand how AI access affects people's willingness to say "I don't know" when facing difficult questions.
AI.method: Conducted five experiments (N=3,132) with four preregistered and one direct replication. Participants answered difficult questions and could always decline to respond. Questions were engineered so AI advice would be wrong, separating AI use from accuracy. Conditions varied AI availability (access vs. no access), whether advice was actively requested or passively displayed, and included incentive manipulations (rewarding accuracy/penalizing inaccuracy).
AI.result: 1) AI access nearly eliminated participants' willingness to suspend judgment, regardless of whether advice was actively requested or simply displayed. 2) Participants answered more questions but were correct about one-third as often compared to when AI was unavailable. 3) Confidence nearly doubled with AI access despite lower accuracy. 4) Accuracy incentives and inaccuracy penalties reduced AI use, improved accuracy, and increased suspension of judgment, though still far less than without AI.
AI.conclusion: AI suggestions, as they become ubiquitous and unsolicited, may not only affect answer accuracy but fundamentally alter the metacognitive threshold at which people decide they know enough to answer. The findings highlight a concerning trade-off where AI access suppresses healthy uncertainty and reduces accuracy while inflating confidence.

title: Agora: Collective and Permissionless Internet-Scale Pretraining of Large Language Models
summary: Training large language models at the multi-billion to trillion parameter scale is confined to datacenters, where data-parallel (DP) and model-parallel (MP) techniques presume homogeneous accelerators, high-speed interconnects, and a single orchestrating entity. Frontier model development is thereby concentrated among the few groups able to assemble such clusters. Meanwhile, an enormous pool of compute remains unusable for training: consumer and professional GPUs that are heterogeneous, preemptible, individually owned, and connected only by the internet. We present Agora, a system that makes efficient use of this compute. Agora combines bandwidth-efficient pipeline-parallel model sharding over internet-grade links with multi-party, fault-tolerant collective operations. Each participant holds only one stage of the model, and no single party ever possesses the full weights. We term this setup Protocol Learning: it enables collectively trained, collectively owned models, opening a path to open-source frontier training with economic sustainability. This report presents the outcome of a research effort spanning communication-efficient parallelism, asynchronous optimization, and fault-tolerant systems design. It culminates in the first demonstration of its kind: Pluralis-8B, an open, permissionless pretraining run of an 8.6B-parameter model on 500B tokens of FineWeb-Edu. The model was trained over 40 days by 330 contributor nodes, predominantly consumer GPUs on internet connections, joining and leaving throughout. The run sustained ~170k tokens/s and 4.2 tokens per TFLOP of pooled compute, 63% of the efficiency of a centralized H100 baseline, and converged to within a small margin of a centralized reference run.

AI.tldr: Agora enables decentralized training of large language models across heterogeneous consumer GPUs via protocol learning, achieving 63% efficiency of centralized baselines.
AI.motivation: Current large-scale LLM training is limited to data centers with homogeneous hardware, high-speed networks, and centralized control, concentrating power among few organizations while leaving massive distributed compute resources (consumer/professional GPUs) unused due to heterogeneity, preemptibility, individual ownership, and internet connectivity constraints.
AI.method: Agora combines bandwidth-efficient pipeline-parallel model sharding over internet links with multi-party, fault-tolerant collective operations using Protocol Learning, where each participant holds only one stage of the model and no single party possesses full weights.
AI.result: Demonstrated with Pluralis-8B, an 8.6B-parameter model trained on 500B tokens over 40 days by 330 contributor nodes using predominantly consumer GPUs, achieving ~170k tokens/s, 4.2 tokens/TFLOP (63% of centralized H100 efficiency), converging close to centralized reference performance.
AI.conclusion: Protocol Learning enables collectively trained, collectively owned models, opening a path to open-source frontier training with economic sustainability by leveraging distributed heterogeneous compute resources previously unusable for large-scale model training.

title: SAFETY SENTRY: Context-Aware Human Intervention via EXECUTE-ASK-REFUSE Routing
summary: LLM agents act on real-world environments through tool calls, and a single misjudged action can cause irreversible harm. The standard safeguard is a guard model that labels each proposed action as safe or unsafe, but this binary view conflates two distinct decisions: whether the action is harmful in itself, and whether it is appropriate given the user's context. It also operates at the granularity of action categories rather than individual instances, producing routine interruptions that erode autonomy and train users to wave through the most consequential alerts. We reframe the problem as a per-instance three-way routing decision over {EXECUTE, ASK, REFUSE} and instantiate it with Safety Sentry, a lightweight guard model whose inference reduces to a single decoding call. A single decoding-time threshold lets one fixed checkpoint be re-positioned across deployments of differing risk tolerance without retraining. Safety Sentry outperforms a broad set of open-weight and frontier closed-source baselines on overall accuracy and safety-related recall, while controlling both directional error rates simultaneously.

AI.tldr: Safety Sentry reframes AI safety as a three-way routing decision (EXECUTE/ASK/REFUSE) instead of binary safe/unsafe classification, using a lightweight guard model with single decoding call inference and adjustable risk tolerance.
AI.motivation: Current guard models for LLM agents use binary safe/unsafe classification which conflates two issues: whether an action is inherently harmful versus contextually appropriate. This binary approach operates at action category level rather than per-instance, causing frequent routine interruptions that erode user autonomy and lead to alert fatigue where users may wave through important safety warnings.
AI.method: Safety Sentry introduces a three-way routing decision over {EXECUTE, ASK, REFUSE} for each action instance. It uses a lightweight guard model with inference reduced to a single decoding call. A key innovation is a single decoding-time threshold that allows a fixed checkpoint to be repositioned across different deployments with varying risk tolerance levels without requiring retraining.
AI.result: Safety Sentry outperforms a broad set of open-weight and frontier closed-source baselines on overall accuracy and safety-related recall, while simultaneously controlling both directional error rates (false positives and false negatives).
AI.conclusion: The three-way routing approach provides more nuanced safety decisions than binary classification, reduces unnecessary interruptions while maintaining safety, and offers practical deployment flexibility through adjustable risk thresholds without model retraining. This represents a significant improvement over current guard model approaches for LLM agent safety.

title: Weight Feedback Computes the Jacobian Transpose Locally in Modern Deep Networks
summary: Predictive Coding (PC) offers a biologically motivated alternative to backpropagation via local weight updates, yet routing error between layers still relies on an autograd Jacobian-transpose ($J^\top$) product - the last non-local operation in PC. We show that this dependency is largely avoidable. For any layer $f(x)=\mathrm{Act}(\mathrm{Norm}(L(x)))$ with frozen normalization statistics, the exact $J^\top$ factors into three locally available terms, $J^\top v = L^\top(s \odot σ'(z) \odot v)$, where $σ'$ is the activation derivative, $z$ is the pre-activation, and $s=γ/σ_{\mathrm{run}}$ is the normalization gain. Prior weight-feedback methods omitted both corrections; restoring them closes the transport gap for this layer class. Locality here holds up to three assumptions, which we state upfront: weight symmetry ($L^\top$ mirrors the forward operator, as assumed by all PC), a soft spectral-norm control that is not synapse-local, and a nearest-neighbour approximation for MaxPool. Substituting the identity into PC yields WF-Act-PC, which removes the autograd backward pass from error transport. On CIFAR-10/100 (50 epochs, 5 seeds), WF-Act-PC is the only PC method whose accuracy improves with depth, surpassing iPC - the strongest classical PC baseline - by 2.7-22.3 pp on CIFAR-10. With both methods tuned per architecture, it matches or exceeds a comparably-tuned backpropagation baseline on the deeper CIFAR-10 architectures (VGG-9: 93.57% vs. 92.43%; ResNet-18: 92.76% vs. 91.54%) and on the harder Tiny-ImageNet benchmark, while trailing tuned BP on the deeper CIFAR-100 VGG cells. Our WF-Act-PC implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/jlshen025/pcax

AI.tldr: Predictive Coding can eliminate the last non-local operation (autograd Jacobian-transpose) by factorizing it into locally available terms for layers with frozen normalization, enabling weight-feedback PC that improves with depth and matches/beats backpropagation on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Predictive Coding offers a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation but still relies on a non-local autograd Jacobian-transpose operation for error routing between layers. The authors aim to remove this dependency to make PC fully local and biologically plausible.
AI.method: The authors show that for layers with frozen normalization statistics, the exact Jacobian-transpose can be factorized into three locally available terms: L^⊤(s ⊙ σ'(z) ⊙ v), where σ' is activation derivative, z is pre-activation, and s is normalization gain. This leads to WF-Act-PC which removes autograd from error transport. The method requires weight symmetry, soft spectral-norm control, and nearest-neighbor approximation for MaxPool.
AI.result: WF-Act-PC is the only PC method whose accuracy improves with depth, surpassing iPC (strongest classical PC baseline) by 2.7-22.3 percentage points on CIFAR-10. It matches or exceeds comparably-tuned backpropagation on deeper CIFAR-10 architectures (VGG-9: 93.57% vs 92.43%; ResNet-18: 92.76% vs 91.54%) and on Tiny-ImageNet, while trailing on deeper CIFAR-100 VGG cells.
AI.conclusion: The dependency on autograd Jacobian-transpose in Predictive Coding is largely avoidable. By factorizing it into locally available terms for layers with frozen normalization, the authors create WF-Act-PC that eliminates the last non-local operation, making PC more biologically plausible while achieving competitive performance with backpropagation on several benchmarks.

title: Automatic Ordinary Differential Equations Discovery For Biological Systems Using Large Language Model Powered Agentic System
summary: Automatic scientific discovery has long been a goal of computational scholars - a machine that can discover nature's secrets on its own, moving computational systems beyond data-fitting tools toward the generation and refinement of mechanistic models of the universe. Recent advances in symbolic regression (SR) and large-language-model (LLM)-based agents suggest that such systems can recover equations from data, incorporate domain priors, and automate parts of the research workflow. However, most existing approaches either focus on narrow equation-discovery benchmarks or broad end-to-end automation pipelines, while biological systems remain comparatively underexplored. Here, we introduce the MEDA system, an LLM- and SR-powered agentic framework for discovering ordinary-differential-equation (ODE) models of biological and biologically inspired dynamical systems. MEDA retrieves background knowledge, defines admissible variables, generates mechanistic constraints, proposes candidate ODEs, and fits and evaluates them. We evaluate it across canonical model retrieval, reasoning-based extrapolation to unseen variants, and open-ended discovery, with and without experimental data. Across these settings, MEDA recovered the correct state variables, achieved strong structural recovery in retrieval and extrapolation tasks, and produced biologically plausible discovery-oriented models. Ablation and robustness analyses show that knowledge-guided formalization and mechanistic constraints are load-bearing components, whereas numerical fitting alone can preserve trajectory-compatible but biologically incorrect equations.

AI.tldr: MEDA is an LLM and symbolic regression agentic framework that discovers ODE models of biological systems, combining knowledge retrieval, mechanistic constraints, and numerical fitting to automate scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: There is a need to move beyond narrow equation-discovery benchmarks and create systems that can automatically discover mechanistic models of complex biological systems, which remain underexplored compared to traditional SR and LLM approaches focused on simpler equations or broad automation pipelines.
AI.method: MEDA uses LLM-powered agents to retrieve background knowledge, define admissible variables, generate mechanistic constraints, propose candidate ODEs, and then applies symbolic regression for fitting and evaluation. The framework is tested across canonical model retrieval, reasoning-based extrapolation to unseen variants, and open-ended discovery tasks.
AI.result: MEDA successfully recovered correct state variables, achieved strong structural recovery in retrieval and extrapolation tasks, and produced biologically plausible discovery-oriented models. The system demonstrated that knowledge-guided formalization and mechanistic constraints are essential components, while numerical fitting alone can preserve trajectory-compatible but biologically incorrect equations.
AI.conclusion: The MEDA framework represents an important step toward automatic scientific discovery in biological systems by effectively combining LLM-based knowledge integration with symbolic regression, showing particular promise for mechanistic model discovery where domain knowledge and biological plausibility are crucial.

title: Where Should RL Post-Training Compute Go? Model Size, Search, Learning, and Feedback
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training is increasingly used to adapt foundation models for reasoning, planning, and feedback-driven robot-learning pipelines, but constrained post-training resources are often summarized by a single total FLOP budget. We study the fixed-budget decision problem behind this practice: under the same post-training budget, should one use a larger policy, train a smaller policy longer, generate more rollout search, or spend compute on stronger reward feedback? We introduce a FLOP-accounting framework for GRPO post-training that decomposes compute into rollout/search, policy-update/learning, and reward- or feedback-model evaluation. Across LoRA-adapted Qwen2.5 policies, we find conditional allocation frontiers: the best observed allocation changes with model size, compute budget, reward system, and evaluation target. Same-FLOP model-size comparisons show that model choice and training allocation are coupled because larger policies consume more per-token compute and therefore buy fewer updates or rollouts under the same budget. Reward systems also change the accounting: rule-based rewards spend nearly all non-update compute on policy rollouts, while PRM-style feedback allocates a visible part of the budget to reward-model inference. We present RACE as a diagnostic pilot-grid protocol, not a guarantee of held-out improvement, for identifying allocation regimes before expensive validation runs; our results suggest that RL post-training papers should report total FLOPs together with how compute is divided among model size, search, learning, and feedback.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes compute allocation trade-offs in RL post-training for foundation models, showing that under fixed FLOP budgets, optimal allocation depends on model size, budget, reward system, and evaluation target.
AI.motivation: RL post-training is increasingly used to adapt foundation models for reasoning and planning tasks, but practitioners face a fundamental compute allocation problem: given a fixed FLOP budget, should resources be allocated to larger models, longer training, more rollout search, or stronger reward feedback? Current practice often only reports total FLOPs without breakdowns.
AI.method: The authors introduce a FLOP-accounting framework for GRPO post-training that decomposes compute into three components: rollout/search, policy-update/learning, and reward/feedback-model evaluation. They conduct systematic experiments across LoRA-adapted Qwen2.5 policies using RACE, a diagnostic pilot-grid protocol, to identify optimal allocation regimes.
AI.result: Results show conditional allocation frontiers where optimal allocation changes with model size, compute budget, reward system, and evaluation target. Larger models consume more per-token compute, reducing available updates/rollouts under same budget. Rule-based rewards allocate most non-update compute to rollouts, while PRM-style feedback spends significant compute on reward-model inference.
AI.conclusion: RL post-training papers should report not just total FLOPs but also how compute is divided among model size, search, learning, and feedback. The RACE protocol helps identify allocation regimes before expensive validation runs, though it's diagnostic rather than guaranteeing held-out improvement.

title: STOCKTAKE: Measuring the Gap Between Perception and Action in LLM Agents with a Fair Oracle
summary: LLM agents are increasingly evaluated on multi-week decision tasks in which the state that drives cost is never directly observed. On such tasks the final cost cannot say why an agent failed: it may have misread the world, or read it correctly and still failed to act (the knowing-doing gap). Existing evaluations cannot separate these two failures; their reference policies either read privileged information the agent never sees, or are missing altogether. We introduce STOCKTAKE, a 26-week supply-chain replenishment benchmark built as a factored partially observable Markov decision process with six hidden factor processes, designed so that a fair reference policy is computable: an exact Bayes filter per factor drives a rollout policy on the identical observation stream the agent receives. Scoring each run between a symptom-blind base-stock floor (0) and this oracle (1) yields a skill score, and grading each week's written rationale yields a stated-belief detection lag and a knowing-doing rate, so state estimation and control are measured separately. On fifty seeds with curated stress profiles, Claude Sonnet 5, GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, and Grok 4.5 detect 84-88% of hidden failures, typically within a week of onset, yet span skill scores from 0.62 to -0.23: two of the four end below the symptom-blind floor while naming factors slightly faster than the two that beat it. The failure has two faces. Where stress persists, 34-43% of correctly diagnosed stress weeks still end in stockout for every model, a rate that partly reflects the severity of the weeks models notice. That rate also runs opposite to skill: the two models under the floor stock out least on diagnosed weeks, so under-response is only one face of the gap, and their traces point to the other, responses whose cost exceeds what they protect. STOCKTAKE measures both directions of that failure.

AI.tldr: STOCKTAKE is a new supply-chain POMDP benchmark that separately measures state estimation and control in LLM agents, revealing that accurate detection of hidden factors doesn't guarantee good actions; some models perform worse than a naive baseline despite faster diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of LLM agents on long-term decision tasks under partial observability cannot distinguish between two critical failure modes: (1) misreading the world (state estimation error) and (2) failing to act correctly even with accurate beliefs (the knowing-doing gap). Existing reference policies either use privileged information the agent never sees or are absent entirely, making it impossible to isolate these failures.
AI.method: STOCKTAKE is a 26-week supply-chain replenishment benchmark modeled as a factored partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) with six hidden factor processes. A fair reference policy is computed using an exact Bayes filter per factor, driving a rollout policy on the same observation stream the agent receives. Performance is scored on a skill scale from 0 (symptom-blind base-stock policy) to 1 (oracle reference). Additionally, each week's written rationale is graded to produce a stated-belief detection lag and a knowing-doing rate, enabling separate measurement of state estimation and control.
AI.result: On fifty seeds with curated stress profiles, four LLMs (Claude Sonnet 5, GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V4-Pro, and Grok 4.5) detected 84-88% of hidden failures, typically within a week of onset. However, skill scores ranged widely from 0.62 to -0.23, with two models performing below the symptom-blind floor despite diagnosing factors slightly faster than the two that beat it. In stress weeks that were correctly diagnosed, 34-43% still ended in stockout across all models. Notably, models with lower skill scores actually stocked out less on diagnosed weeks, indicating that under-response is only one aspect of the knowing-doing gap; the other is taking actions whose cost exceeds the protection they provide.
AI.conclusion: STOCKTAKE is a novel benchmark that successfully separates and measures state estimation failures (misreading the world) from control failures (knowing-doing gap) in LLM agents performing long-term decision tasks under partial observability. It reveals that even when agents accurately detect hidden factors, their resulting actions can be suboptimal—sometimes even worse than a naive baseline—highlighting the need for evaluations that assess both belief accuracy and action quality independently.

title: Self-Improving is Often Sudden: Enlightenment-style Finetuning for Large-Scale Models
summary: The pursuit of autonomously self-improving models has attracted growing interest in the era of large-scale foundation models. Drawing inspiration from the concept of "enlightenment" or "aha moment" in human brain, we hypothesize that large models exhibit an analogous enlightenment phenomenon-a latent capacity for sudden capability boost. Then, we propose Enlightenment, a novel training-free post-tuning paradigm for large-scale models. Our approach modifies shortcuts for key modules/layers without weight updates, while existing training-free ones predominantly manipulate attention weights. We introduce two architecture-specific instantiations: i) For large language models, we propose attention head-mixing shortcuts that recalibrate attention weights by linking the initial attention head's output to all other target heads, modulated by an adaptive scaling factor initialization strategy. ii) For vision-language models, we apply a lightweight scalar-modulated factor to residual connections in the decoder layers, regulating information flow. Extensive experiments show that Enlightenment efficiently unlocks the latent potential of pre-trained networks, yielding remarkable performance improvements across diverse benchmarks and models.

AI.tldr: Enlightenment: A training-free post-tuning method that unlocks latent potential in large models by modifying shortcuts without weight updates, showing significant performance improvements across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The pursuit of autonomously self-improving models, inspired by the "enlightenment" or "aha moment" phenomenon in human brains where there's a latent capacity for sudden capability boost in large models.
AI.method: A novel training-free post-tuning paradigm that modifies shortcuts for key modules/layers without weight updates. Two architecture-specific instantiations: 1) For LLMs: attention head-mixing shortcuts that recalibrate attention weights by linking initial attention head's output to other heads with adaptive scaling factor initialization. 2) For VLMs: lightweight scalar-modulated factor applied to residual connections in decoder layers to regulate information flow.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Enlightenment efficiently unlocks latent potential of pre-trained networks, yielding remarkable performance improvements across diverse benchmarks and models.
AI.conclusion: Enlightenment provides an effective training-free approach for post-tuning large-scale models that can trigger sudden capability improvements analogous to human enlightenment moments, outperforming existing training-free methods that mainly manipulate attention weights.

title: UESF-Bench: Benchmarking and Probing for Unified Embodied Seeking and Following
summary: Language-guided human following is an important capability for embodied agents, but existing benchmarks typically assume that the target person is visible at the start of an episode. This setting simplifies the problem and overlooks a more realistic requirement: an agent often needs to first find a language-described target and then persistently follow that target in a dynamic environment. While recent work has started to study human search, existing settings are typically evaluated in task-specific scenarios and often rely on stronger prior knowledge of the environment. Moreover, they usually treat searching and following as separate tasks and still lack a unified benchmark for systematic evaluation. To address these limitations, we introduce the Unified Embodied Seeking and Following Benchmark (UESF-Bench), a large-scale and diverse benchmark for embodied human seeking and following. The benchmark requires agents to handle semantic-guided exploration, reliable behavior switching and recovery, and delayed identity grounding. To this end, we propose SeekFollow-VLA, a vision-language-action framework with a task-driven routing mechanism for latent phase inference and transition modeling between seeking and following. Experimental results show that SeekFollow-VLA achieves clear improvements over both single-head and dual-head baselines across single-person and multi-person environments, establishing a baseline for unified embodied seek-and-follow.

AI.tldr: Proposes UESF-Bench, a unified benchmark for embodied human seeking and following, along with SeekFollow-VLA framework that handles both search and follow tasks with task routing.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks assume target person is initially visible, which oversimplifies real-world scenarios. Current work treats searching and following as separate tasks, lacks unified evaluation, and often relies on strong environmental priors.
AI.method: Introduces UESF-Bench as a large-scale, diverse benchmark for embodied human seeking and following. Proposes SeekFollow-VLA, a vision-language-action framework with task-driven routing mechanism for latent phase inference and transition modeling between seeking and following states.
AI.result: Experimental results show SeekFollow-VLA achieves clear improvements over single-head and dual-head baselines across both single-person and multi-person environments, establishing a strong baseline for unified seek-and-follow tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work addresses limitations in existing benchmarks by providing a unified evaluation framework for realistic embodied human seeking and following, with a proposed method that effectively handles semantic exploration, behavior switching, and delayed identity grounding.

title: Is the Statistical Advantage Worth the Cost? An Empirical Comparison of KANs and MLPs for Structured Data Classification
comment: To be presented at the 9th International Conference on Machine Learning and Machine Intelligence (MLMI 2026), Tokyo, Japan
summary: This study presents an empirical benchmarking comparison between Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) on structured tabular classification tasks. Motivated by the growing interest in KANs as an alternative function-approximating architecture, we evaluate their out-of-the-box performance on twelve publicly available datasets spanning binary, multiclass, multilabel, and ordinal problems. Both models were trained under standardized preprocessing, architecture, and fixed hyperparameter settings, with performance assessed using test accuracy and F1-Score, paired hypothesis testing, and effect size analysis. Results show that KANs statistically outperform MLPs in binary and multiclass domains and achieve a significant aggregate advantage across all datasets.
