AI.conclusion: PiVoT provides an effective training-free solution for multi-object detection and tracking in noisy radar point clouds that addresses limitations of current Bayesian trackers while achieving state-of-the-art performance and real-time operation.

title: RF Spectrogram Anomaly Detection with Quantum Kitchen Sinks: Architecture, Representation, and Hardware Validation
comment: Paper accepted to IEEE quantum week 2026
summary: The broadcast nature of wireless channels exposes radio-frequency (RF) networks to anomalous and malicious transmissions, making anomaly detection a fundamental requirement for secure spectrum management. Quantum Kitchen Sinks (QKS) offer a lightweight hybrid quantum feature map suitable for near-term quantum devices, yet their behavior on structured signal data remains poorly understood. In this paper, we extend the standard QKS template with multi-depth data re-uploading and ring entanglement, and evaluate the resulting pipeline on controlled RF spectrogram anomaly detection. We introduce a validation-locked five-stage ablation protocol that systematically separates the effects of shallow architecture, re-uploading depth, episode budget, input representation, and classical readout. Across the completed benchmark, Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) representations consistently dominate raw and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) inputs, moderate-depth entangled QKS configurations form the strongest operating regime, and QKS improves over matched classical direct-readout baselines across all evaluated representation-readout pairs on the held-out test set, with the best configuration reaching a test Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.8778 and a test F1 of 0.7995. The study bridges two levels of realism: real measured sub-6\,GHz cellular signals on the data side and real-device validation on the ibm_quebec Quantum Processing Unit (QPU) on the computing side, with AUROC deviations below 0.013 relative to simulation. These results provide a practical, reproducible framework for deploying QKS-based anomaly detection in wireless networks.

AI.tldr: QKS with multi-depth data re-uploading and ring entanglement improves RF anomaly detection, achieving test AUROC of 0.8778 with real wireless data and real quantum device validation.
AI.motivation: Wireless RF networks are vulnerable to anomalous/malicious transmissions, requiring secure spectrum management. Quantum Kitchen Sinks (QKS) are promising for near-term quantum devices but their behavior on structured signal data is poorly understood.
AI.method: Extended standard QKS template with multi-depth data re-uploading and ring entanglement, evaluated on RF spectrogram anomaly detection using a validation-locked five-stage ablation protocol that separates effects of shallow architecture, re-uploading depth, episode budget, input representation, and classical readout.
AI.result: DCT representations dominated raw and PCA inputs, moderate-depth entangled QKS formed strongest operating regime. QKS outperformed classical baselines across all representation-readout pairs, with best configuration achieving test AUROC of 0.8778 and test F1 of 0.7995. Real-device validation on ibm_quebec QPU showed AUROC deviations below 0.013 from simulation.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a practical, reproducible framework for deploying QKS-based anomaly detection in wireless networks, bridging data realism (real sub-6GHz cellular signals) with computing realism (real quantum device validation).

title: An Efficient Newton Algorithm for Nonnegative Matrix Factorization with the Kullback-Leibler Divergence
summary: Nonnegative Matrix Factorization (NMF) is a fundamental tool in unsupervised learning, which approximates a nonnegative matrix by the product of two low-rank nonnegative factors. The Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence is best suited to measure the data to model discrepancy when the decomposed data sample follows a Poisson distribution, which is the case for count datasets such as term-document matrices or images. Most KL-NMF algorithms in the literature minimize a separable majorant of the loss to find their next iterate. We argue that this method has reached its limits and propose to use instead the second-order Taylor expansion of the loss, leading to a Newton-type method. We minimize this non-separable surrogate by proposing a generalization of the well-known HALS algorithm. This yields an efficient KL-NMF algorithm which provably converges and which competes favorably with state-of-the-art algorithms on a large variety of datasets.

AI.tldr: A new Newton-type algorithm for KL-divergence Nonnegative Matrix Factorization using second-order Taylor expansion, generalizing HALS for better convergence and performance on count datasets.
AI.motivation: Current KL-NMF algorithms using separable majorants have reached their limits, and KL divergence is best suited for count datasets following Poisson distributions (like term-document matrices or images).
AI.method: Uses second-order Taylor expansion of the loss (Newton-type method) instead of separable majorants, and generalizes the HALS algorithm to minimize this non-separable surrogate.
AI.result: Proposed algorithm provably converges and competes favorably with state-of-the-art algorithms on a large variety of datasets.
AI.conclusion: The new Newton-type approach with generalized HALS yields an efficient KL-NMF algorithm that improves upon existing methods, particularly for count data applications.

title: VAIOM: Continuous-Input, Discrete-Output Decoder-Only Financial Sequence Modeling
summary: Financial observations are continuous, heterogeneous, and noisy, whereas decoder-only next-token models are usually built around discrete symbolic inputs. We introduce Vector-Input Autoregressive Inference for Ordinal-Return Modeling (VAIOM), a decoder-only Transformer for probabilistic next-return modeling on one-hour foreign-exchange bars. VAIOM separates input representation from output likelihood: continuous multivariate financial-event vectors preserve numerical structure at the input, while a categorical distribution over the next volatility-normalized return bucket supports cross-entropy training and likelihood evaluation. The selected 0.9M Hybrid Continuous Input model combines continuous event features with categorical asset metadata, a Mixture-of-Market-States return head, Gap, volatility-regime, and Ordinal auxiliary objectives, and full-sequence supervision. Models and preprocessing are fit using pre-2024 Train data; models are selected on 2024H2 Validation and evaluated without refitting on two 2025 Test periods. Across three independent training seeds, every model outperforms fixed single-bar LightGBM baseline in both Test halves. For the canonical checkpoint, paired gains over LightGBM are 0.029 and 0.043 bits per event. Validation experiments show that continuous input improves over discrete-token input under the same categorical return objective, full-sequence supervision improves over last-position training, and auxiliary representation shaping together with a mixture-structured return head improves return likelihood in controlled comparisons. A supporting capacity study finds that the smallest evaluated complete architecture rung achieves the strongest Validation likelihood on the present corpus.

AI.tldr: VAIOM is a decoder-only Transformer for financial return prediction that uses continuous vector inputs instead of discrete tokens, with a categorical output distribution over volatility-normalized return buckets, achieving better performance than traditional baseline models.
AI.motivation: Financial data is continuous, heterogeneous, and noisy, but existing decoder-only models typically use discrete symbolic inputs which don't preserve the numerical structure of financial observations. There's a need for models that can handle continuous financial event vectors while supporting probabilistic modeling of returns.
AI.method: VAIOM separates input representation (continuous multivariate financial-event vectors) from output likelihood (categorical distribution over next volatility-normalized return buckets). The model combines continuous event features with categorical asset metadata, uses a Mixture-of-Market-States return head, and incorporates Gap, volatility-regime, and Ordinal auxiliary objectives with full-sequence supervision.
AI.result: Across three training seeds, every VAIOM model outperforms the fixed single-bar LightGBM baseline in both test periods (2025). The canonical checkpoint shows paired gains of 0.029 and 0.043 bits per event over LightGBM. Validation experiments demonstrate continuous input outperforms discrete-token input, full-sequence supervision beats last-position training, and auxiliary objectives improve return likelihood.
AI.conclusion: VAIOM successfully bridges the gap between continuous financial data and decoder-only Transformer architectures, showing that continuous input representation, full-sequence supervision, and carefully designed auxiliary objectives can significantly improve probabilistic financial return modeling compared to traditional approaches.

title: TRACE: Turn-level Reward Assignment via Credit Estimation for Long-Horizon Agents
summary: Multi-turn agents solve complex tasks through extended sequences of tool interactions before producing a final answer, making credit assignment a fundamental challenge during post-training. Outcome rewards provide reliable supervision for short-horizon reasoning, but become sparse and high-variance as trajectories grow to tens or hundreds of tool calls. They can also be misleading: a failed rollout may contain many useful actions that move the agent closer to the goal, yet outcome-only training assigns them the same negative advantage as the eventual mistake. We propose TRACE (Turn-level Reward Assignment via Credit Estimation), a dense credit-assignment method for agentic reinforcement learning. TRACE represents rollouts as state transitions at tool-call boundaries, obtains gold-answer log-probabilities from a frozen reference model, transforms them into log-ratio state values, and derives per-action rewards as Temporal-Difference changes in those values. This requires no additional critic or process-label training, and its one-step log-ratio TD component telescopes across redundant tool calls. On long-horizon complex search, TRACE substantially improves base-model tool-use ability using pure RL, without a cold-start supervised fine-tuning stage, an agentic mid-training stage, or training on live-web data. On the closed-web BrowseComp-Plus benchmark, it raises Qwen3-4B from $7.2$ to $35.6$ and Qwen3-30B-A3B from $8.4$ to $42.6$. The learned search behavior also transfers to open-web benchmarks, and the learning curves show earlier improvement and faster convergence during RL training.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a dense credit assignment method for multi-turn agents that uses frozen reference model log-probabilities to compute per-action rewards via temporal difference, enabling effective RL training for long-horizon tool use without cold-start SFT or live-web data.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn agents with long trajectories (tens/hundreds of tool calls) face credit assignment challenges. Outcome rewards become sparse/high-variance and misleading - a failed rollout may contain many useful actions but gets same negative advantage as the eventual mistake.
AI.method: TRACE represents rollouts as state transitions at tool-call boundaries, obtains gold-answer log-probabilities from a frozen reference model, transforms them into log-ratio state values, and derives per-action rewards as Temporal-Difference changes in those values.
AI.result: On BrowseComp-Plus benchmark: Qwen3-4B improved from 7.2 to 35.6 and Qwen3-30B-A3B from 8.4 to 42.6. Learned search behavior transfers to open-web benchmarks. Learning curves show earlier improvement and faster convergence during RL training.
AI.conclusion: TRACE enables effective pure RL training for long-horizon agentic tasks without cold-start SFT, agentic mid-training, or live-web data. The method provides dense credit assignment that recognizes useful actions in failed rollouts and handles redundant tool calls.

title: Lyapunov Exponent as Physics-Informed Dense Reward: RL Discovery of Stabilization Beyond the Kapitza Pendulum
summary: We suggest using the Lyapunov characteristic exponent (LCE) as a dense reward signal for the reinforcement learning problem of stabilizing the inverted pendulum with vertical motion. With LCE, the agent not only successfully found the oscillatory motion known as the Kapitza pendulum but also damped the pendulum's pivoting, leaving it in a strictly upright position.

AI.tldr: Using Lyapunov characteristic exponents as dense reward signals enables RL agents to both discover Kapitza pendulum oscillations and achieve strict upright stabilization.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the reinforcement learning challenge of stabilizing inverted pendulums with vertical motion, seeking better reward signals that can guide agents to discover complex stabilization behaviors.
AI.method: Proposes using Lyapunov characteristic exponents (LCE) as dense reward signals for reinforcement learning agents controlling inverted pendulums with vertical motion.
AI.result: The RL agent successfully discovered the oscillatory Kapitza pendulum motion and achieved strict upright stabilization by damping pivoting movements.
AI.conclusion: LCE provides an effective dense reward signal for RL in pendulum stabilization problems, enabling agents to find both oscillatory solutions and achieve perfect upright stabilization.

title: Lighthouse RL: Sample-Efficient Circuit Optimization via Strategic Reset Points
summary: In this paper, we introduce Lighthouse RL, a sample-efficient reinforcement learning (RL) approach for analog circuit sizing. Traditional methods lack generalization across different performance targets, while standard RL approaches waste resources exploring unpromising regions. Our method addresses these inefficiencies through a strategic reset strategy that initializes episodes from high-performing configurations discovered during training, called "lighthouses". These states, which are closer to the target objectives, guide exploration toward promising regions. When compared to RL and Bayesian optimization methods from the literature, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on a 2D benchmark problem and on two analog circuits, showing significant improvements in sample efficiency (up to 1.72x faster), optimization performance (100% vs. 0-87% success rate), generalization (75% vs. 0-50% extrapolation success), and objective maximization. This efficiency is particularly valuable for computationally expensive black-box optimization problems, and our reset strategy can be used as a plug-and-play enhancement for any RL-based optimization approach.

AI.tldr: Lighthouse RL is a sample-efficient reinforcement learning method for analog circuit sizing that uses strategic resets from high-performing "lighthouse" states to guide exploration, achieving significantly better performance than traditional RL and Bayesian optimization.
AI.motivation: Traditional analog circuit sizing methods lack generalization across different performance targets, and standard RL approaches waste resources exploring unpromising regions, leading to inefficiencies in computationally expensive black-box optimization problems.
AI.method: The approach introduces a strategic reset strategy that initializes episodes from high-performing configurations discovered during training (called "lighthouses"). These states are closer to target objectives and guide exploration toward promising regions, acting as a plug-and-play enhancement for any RL-based optimization approach.
AI.result: The method demonstrates significant improvements on a 2D benchmark problem and two analog circuits: 1.72x faster sample efficiency, 100% vs. 0-87% success rate for optimization performance, and 75% vs. 0-50% extrapolation success for generalization, plus better objective maximization.
AI.conclusion: Lighthouse RL offers a highly effective and sample-efficient approach for analog circuit sizing optimization, with the strategic reset strategy providing valuable efficiency gains for computationally expensive black-box problems that can enhance any RL-based optimization method.

title: MetaPerch: Learning from metadata for bioacoustics foundation models
summary: Bioacoustic foundation models rely on large-scale citizen science platforms like Xeno-Canto for geographically and ecologically diverse data. Recent work has shown that supervision alone can produce SotA species detection models when trained on this large-scale data -- however, there remains unutilized potential in the form of recording metadata readily available within these community-driven data hubs. In this work, we explore the use of metadata -- such as location and time -- as auxiliary supervision signals, allowing the model to leverage species-metadata correlations in its learned representation. Auxiliary metadata losses provide additional information beyond vocalizations alone that can encourage a richer, more robust representation that generalizes better to species distribution and acoustic domain shifts -- important challenges for deployment in real-world passive acoustic monitoring (PAM) settings. We introduce MetaPerch, a new foundation model that achieves strong species identification performance across multiple challenging domains and present an extensive empirical study of the effects of 9 diverse metadata sources on 17 bioacoustic datasets.

AI.tldr: MetaPerch is a bioacoustic foundation model that uses recording metadata (location, time, etc.) as auxiliary supervision signals alongside species labels to improve generalization and robustness in passive acoustic monitoring.
AI.motivation: While large-scale citizen science platforms like Xeno-Canto provide diverse bioacoustic data, current models only use species supervision and ignore readily available metadata. There's untapped potential in leveraging species-metadata correlations to improve model robustness against domain shifts in real-world passive acoustic monitoring scenarios.
AI.method: The authors develop MetaPerch, a foundation model that incorporates 9 diverse metadata sources (location, time, etc.) as auxiliary supervision signals. These metadata losses provide additional information beyond vocalizations alone, encouraging richer representations that better capture species distribution patterns and acoustic domain variations.
AI.result: MetaPerch achieves strong species identification performance across multiple challenging domains. The paper presents an extensive empirical study showing the effects of 9 metadata sources on 17 different bioacoustic datasets, demonstrating improved generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating metadata as auxiliary supervision significantly enhances bioacoustic foundation models by leveraging species-metadata correlations, resulting in more robust representations that generalize better to real-world passive acoustic monitoring settings with domain shifts.

title: Linear Independent Component Analysis via Optimal Transport
summary: Linear Independent Component Analysis (ICA) recovers jointly independent source signals from their linear mixtures. To achieve this, classical ICA algorithms attempt to maximize non-Gaussianity, measured by negentropy, which is linked to independence by information theory. Because exact negentropy optimization is intractable, they rely on proxy contrast functions, such as fourth-order cumulants, and parametric log-likelihoods. We propose instead to measure non-Gaussianity using the squared Wasserstein distance $W_2^2$ to a standard Gaussian. We prove that the Wasserstein distance between a standard normal distribution and linear projections of the data is maximized when the projection recovers an independent component. Based on this observation, we propose the OT-ICA algorithm which finds this projection by gradient-based optimization. Empirical evaluation on simulated data shows that OT-ICA outperforms proxy-based methods for different distributions of the latent variables. Application to EEG artifact removal and econometric price discovery confirm OT-ICA can be used for applied ICA tasks without distributional assumptions.

AI.tldr: OT-ICA: A new ICA algorithm that uses squared Wasserstein distance instead of traditional proxy contrast functions to find independent components through gradient optimization.
AI.motivation: Traditional ICA algorithms rely on proxy contrast functions (like fourth-order cumulants) to approximate negentropy optimization, which can be limited and require distributional assumptions. There's a need for a more principled approach without these limitations.
AI.method: Proposes OT-ICA algorithm that measures non-Gaussianity using squared Wasserstein distance (W₂²) to a standard Gaussian. Proves this distance is maximized when projection recovers independent components, then finds this projection through gradient-based optimization.
AI.result: OT-ICA outperforms proxy-based methods on simulated data across different latent variable distributions. Successfully applied to EEG artifact removal and econometric price discovery, demonstrating practical utility without distributional assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Wasserstein distance provides a principled alternative to proxy contrast functions for ICA, enabling distribution-free independent component recovery with practical applications in signal processing and econometrics.

title: Leveraging unlabelled data for generalizable neural population decoding
summary: Robust and accurate neural decoders are integral to neurotechnologies such as brain-computer interfaces and closed-loop experiments. Recent work has shown that tokenizing neural data at the spike level facilitates multi-session pretraining and delivers state-of-the-art decoding performance. However, current spike-based models are restricted to supervised learning (SL), limiting training to datasets with paired behavioural labels. To address this limitation, we introduce MOJO (Masked autOencoder-based JOint training), a training framework for spike-tokenizing models that jointly leverages self-supervised learning (SSL) via masked autoencoding and SL objectives. We evaluate MOJO on three spiking datasets spanning monkey motor cortex during reaching tasks and multi-regional mouse recordings during vision and decision making tasks, demonstrating superior performance over purely SL-trained models. This improvement is especially pronounced when training with limited labelled data, particularly in few-shot finetuning, where only a small amount of labelled data from a new session is available. Incorporating SSL also yields more interpretable neuronal representations, improving performance on brain region classification and spike-statistics prediction without explicit optimization for these tasks. We further show that MOJO generalizes beyond spiking data to human electrocorticography during speech, where it continues to outperform purely SL-trained models and achieves performance comparable to neuro-foundation models (NFMs) designed specifically for continuous signals. Overall, augmenting spike-tokenizing models with SSL improves performance in label-impoverished settings and enables the use of unlabelled data across various tasks and species, while generalizing to other neural modalities. These results suggest a path towards more flexible and scalable data usage when training NFMs.

AI.tldr: MOJO is a joint training framework that combines self-supervised learning (masked autoencoding) with supervised learning for spike-based neural decoders, improving performance especially with limited labeled data and generalizing across species and neural modalities.
AI.motivation: Current spike-based neural decoders are restricted to supervised learning, limiting training to datasets with paired behavioral labels. There's a need to leverage unlabeled neural data and improve performance in label-impoverished settings common in neurotechnology applications.
AI.method: MOJO introduces joint training combining SSL via masked autoencoding with SL objectives for spike-tokenizing models. It uses a masked autoencoder framework to leverage self-supervised learning from unlabeled data while maintaining supervised decoding capabilities.
AI.result: MOJO outperforms purely SL-trained models across three spiking datasets (monkey motor cortex, mouse vision/decision making), especially in few-shot finetuning with limited labeled data. It also improves interpretability of neuronal representations and generalizes to human electrocorticography data, achieving performance comparable to neuro-foundation models designed for continuous signals.
AI.conclusion: Augmenting spike-tokenizing models with SSL improves performance in label-impoverished settings, enables use of unlabeled data across tasks and species, and generalizes to other neural modalities, suggesting a path toward more flexible and scalable data usage for training neuro-foundation models.
